onboarding.subtitle: Learn economics in minutes a day

home.dailyChallengeDesc: Complete a lesson and earn bonus XP

lesson.quitMessage: You'll lose your progress in this lesson.

lessons.protect_in_crisis: How to protect yourself in a crisis
lessons.get_out_debt: How to get out of debt

q.wim_1: Which of the following is the primary function of money?
q.wim_1_exp: Money facilitates the exchange of goods and services between people.
q.wim_2: Money must be accepted by both parties in a transaction to work correctly.
q.wim_2_exp: For money to work, both parties must trust its value and accept it as payment.
q.wim_3: How many main functions does money have?
q.wim_3_exp: Money has three functions: medium of exchange, unit of account, and store of value.
q.wim_4: Barter is more efficient than using money.
q.wim_4_exp: Barter requires both parties to want what the other offers. Money eliminates this limitation.
q.wim_5: What problem did money historically solve?
q.wim_5_a: The double coincidence of wants in barter
q.wim_5_exp: Barter required two people to want exactly what the other had. Money eliminates that problem.
q.tom_1_c: Money based on trust and government backing
q.tom_1_exp: Fiat money is not backed by a physical commodity, but by trust in the government that issues it.
q.tom_2: Paper money was always backed by gold.
q.tom_2_exp: Historically there was a gold standard, but the U.S. abandoned it in 1971. Today money is fiat.
q.tom_3: What was the first type of money in history?
q.tom_3_exp: Metal coins (gold, silver, copper) were the first standardized forms of minted money.
q.tom_4: Cryptocurrencies are a type of decentralized digital money.
q.tom_4_exp: Cryptocurrencies like Bitcoin operate without a central bank, using blockchain technology.
q.tom_5: In what year did the U.S. abandon the gold standard?
q.tom_5_exp: In 1971, President Nixon announced the end of the dollar's convertibility to gold, ending the gold standard.
q.pp_1_a: The amount of money you have
q.pp_1_b: The quantity of goods and services you can buy with your money
q.pp_1_exp: Purchasing power is the ability of your money to buy goods and services in the market.
q.pp_2: When inflation rises, the purchasing power of money falls.
q.pp_2_exp: With inflation, prices rise and the same money buys fewer things. That's why purchasing power falls.
q.pp_3: If the price of a basic basket rises from $100 to $110, inflation was:
q.pp_3_exp: Inflation = (new price - old price) / old price × 100 = (110-100)/100 × 100 = 10%.
q.pp_4: A wage increase always improves purchasing power.
q.pp_4_exp: If wages rise 10% but inflation is 20%, purchasing power actually fell. What matters is real wages.
q.pp_5: Which strategy helps protect purchasing power?
q.pp_5_a: Keeping all cash under the mattress
q.pp_5_b: Spending everything as soon as you get paid
q.pp_5_c: Investing in assets that beat inflation
q.pp_5_exp: Investing in assets (stocks, real estate, etc.) that grow above inflation protects purchasing power.
q.mh_1: In which civilization were the first written records of commercial exchange created?
q.mh_1_exp: The Sumerians in Mesopotamia (modern-day Iraq) were the first to record commercial transactions over 5,000 years ago.
q.mh_2: Barter was the exchange system used before the invention of money.
q.mh_2_exp: Before money, people exchanged goods directly. The problem was the 'double coincidence of wants': both parties had to want what the other offered.
q.mh_3: Which country invented paper money for the first time in history?
q.mh_3_exp: China was the first country to use paper money, during the Tang Dynasty (7th century AD), as a practical solution to the weight of metal coins.
q.mh_4: Argentina had more than one national currency throughout the 20th century.
q.mh_4_exp: Argentina went through the peso moneda nacional, peso ley, peso argentino, the austral, and the current peso, reflecting its successive economic crises.
q.mh_5: What was the Argentine currency immediately before the current peso?
q.mh_5_exp: The austral was Argentina's currency from 1985 to 1991. It was replaced by the peso at a rate of 10,000 australes per peso during the convertibility era.
q.inf_1_a: A generalized and sustained increase in prices
q.inf_1_b: A drop in the minimum wage
q.inf_1_c: The growth of the gross domestic product
q.inf_1_exp: Inflation happens when prices of goods and services rise in a sustained way, causing the same money to buy fewer and fewer things over time.
q.inf_2_exp: In 2023, Argentina recorded annual inflation of 211.4% according to the INDEC statistical agency, one of the highest in its recent history.
q.inf_3_a: A sharp drop in the exchange rate
q.inf_3_c: A generalized and sustained decrease in prices
q.inf_3_d: A rise in the minimum wage
q.inf_3_exp: Deflation is the opposite of inflation: prices fall. Although it seems good, it can cause people to stop spending while waiting for even lower prices.
q.inf_4: An annual inflation rate of 3% is considered hyperinflation.
q.inf_4_exp: 3% annual inflation is low and manageable. Hyperinflation involves a very rapid loss of value, generally above 50% per month.
q.inf_5: Which agency officially measures inflation in Argentina?
q.inf_5_exp: The INDEC (National Statistics and Census Institute) publishes the monthly CPI (Consumer Price Index) that measures official inflation in Argentina.
q.ci_1: Which of these is a direct cause of inflation?
q.ci_1_a: Printing more money without backing in the real economy
q.ci_1_exp: When there's more money circulating but the same amount of goods, each unit of currency buys less and prices rise. This is one of the most common causes of inflation.
q.ci_2: Printing more money without productive backing generates inflation.
q.ci_2_exp: Excess money creation without the economy producing more goods makes each unit of currency worth less — more money chasing the same products.
q.ci_3_a: When wages rise faster than inflation
q.ci_3_b: When supply of goods exceeds demand
q.ci_3_c: When demand for goods exceeds available supply
q.ci_3_exp: Demand-pull inflation occurs when everyone wants to buy more than is available. With more buyers than products, prices are pushed upward.
q.ci_4: Cost-push inflation occurs when production inputs become more expensive.
q.ci_4_exp: If oil, energy, or raw material prices rise, companies pass those higher costs on to the final price of their products, creating cost-push inflation.
q.ci_5: What tool does a central bank use to try to lower inflation?
q.ci_5_exp: By raising interest rates, the central bank makes credit more expensive, discouraging spending and investment, which reduces demand pressure that fuels inflation.
q.cpi_1: What does the CPI (Consumer Price Index) measure?
q.cpi_1_a: The price variation of a representative basket of goods and services
q.cpi_1_b: The informal exchange rate of the dollar
q.cpi_1_d: The total public debt of a country
q.cpi_1_exp: The CPI measures how the price of a typical consumption basket (food, transport, housing, etc.) changes over time to calculate inflation.
q.cpi_2: The CPI is used to calculate the official inflation of a country.
q.cpi_2_exp: The CPI is the most widely used indicator to measure inflation: it compares the cost of the same basket of goods across different time periods.
q.cpi_3: Which agency publishes the CPI in Argentina?
q.cpi_3_exp: The INDEC publishes the monthly CPI with data from different regions of the country, covering food, housing, transport, health, education, and more.
q.cpi_4: The CPI basket includes food, transportation, and household services.
q.cpi_4_exp: The CPI considers a broad basket: food and beverages, clothing, housing, household equipment, health, transportation, communication, education and more.
q.cpi_5: If the CPI was 100 in January and rises to 111 in February, what was the monthly inflation?
q.cpi_5_exp: Inflation = (new CPI - old CPI) / old CPI × 100 = (111 - 100) / 100 × 100 = 11% monthly inflation.
q.pb_1_a: A plan that organizes a person's income and expenses
q.pb_1_b: A bank loan for personal spending
q.pb_1_exp: A personal budget is a tool that shows how much you earn, how much you spend, and where. It helps you take control of your money.
q.pb_2: Consistently spending more than you earn leads to accumulating debt.
q.pb_2_exp: If your expenses consistently exceed your income, you will inevitably accumulate debts that can become difficult to manage over time.
q.pb_3: What is the first step to building a personal budget?
q.pb_3_b: Record all your income and expenses for the month
q.pb_3_exp: Before planning, you need to know where you stand. Recording what comes in and goes out is the starting point of any financial plan.
q.pb_4: Personal budgets are only useful for people with high incomes.
q.pb_4_exp: Budgeting is especially useful when resources are limited. Anyone, regardless of income level, benefits from organizing their finances.
q.pb_5: What are fixed expenses in a budget?
q.pb_5_a: Expenses that vary greatly each month
q.pb_5_c: Recurring expenses with the same amount: rent, loan payments, utilities
q.pb_5_exp: Fixed expenses are the easiest to plan because they're always the same: rent, loan installments, internet, monthly subscriptions.
q.r5020_1: In the 50/30/20 rule, what percentage is allocated to basic needs?
q.r5020_1_exp: 50% of income goes to essential needs: housing, food, transport, utilities, and everything you can't avoid paying.
q.r5020_2: According to the 50/30/20 rule, 20% of income should go toward savings and investments.
q.r5020_2_exp: The 20% is dedicated to building your future: emergency fund, savings, investments, or paying off debt.
q.r5020_3: What does the 30% represent in the 50/30/20 rule?
q.r5020_3_exp: The 30% is for your wants and desires: dining out, extra clothing, streaming services, travel, hobbies. It's important to enjoy life — within this limit.
q.r5020_4: The 50/30/20 rule is rigid and cannot be adapted to individual situations.
q.r5020_4_exp: The rule is a flexible guide. If you live in an expensive city or have heavy debts, you can adjust the percentages (e.g., 60/20/20) to fit your reality.
q.r5020_5: If you earn $2,000/month, how much should go to savings according to the 50/30/20 rule?
q.r5020_5_exp: 20% of $2,000 = $400 for savings and investments. 50% ($1,000) for needs and 30% ($600) for wants.
q.ws_1: What is the main benefit of saving money?
q.ws_1_a: Having financial security for emergencies and future goals
q.ws_1_b: Showing off to friends and family
q.ws_1_exp: Saving gives you freedom and security: you can handle unexpected events without falling into debt and plan for important goals like travel, a car, or a home.
q.ws_2: Keeping cash at home is the best way to save in high-inflation environments.
q.ws_2_exp: In high-inflation contexts, cash loses value quickly. Money under the mattress doesn't protect you — you need instruments that beat inflation.
q.ws_3: What is the main difference between saving and investing?
q.ws_3_a: Saving always generates more return than investing
q.ws_3_b: They are exactly the same, just different names
q.ws_3_c: Saving preserves capital with lower risk; investing seeks to grow it by taking on more risk
q.ws_3_exp: Saving is preserving capital safely. Investing is putting that capital to work to grow, but with greater risk. They are complementary, not opposites.
q.ws_4: Saving even 10% of your monthly income is a very valuable financial habit.
q.ws_4_exp: Saving 10% consistently may seem small, but thanks to compound interest and discipline, it can generate significant wealth over the long term.
q.ws_5: To protect savings from inflation, which instrument is most suitable?
q.ws_5_d: Inflation-adjusted savings account or dollar-pegged stablecoin
q.ws_5_exp: Inflation-indexed accounts protect purchasing power. Dollar-pegged stablecoins (USDC, DAI) allow digital dollar savings. Both options protect better than holding cash.
q.ef_1: How many months of expenses should an ideal emergency fund cover?
q.ef_1_exp: Financial experts recommend having 3 to 6 months of expenses covered to handle unexpected events like job loss or a medical emergency.
q.ef_2: An emergency fund should be invested in stocks to grow faster.
q.ef_2_exp: An emergency fund must be immediately accessible. Stocks can crash right when you need the money most. It should be in something liquid and safe.
q.ef_3: What kind of situation is an emergency fund designed for?
q.ef_3_c: Serious unexpected expenses: job loss, illness, urgent repairs
q.ef_3_exp: An emergency fund is exclusively for urgent, unexpected situations that threaten your financial stability. It's not for planned spending.
q.ef_4: An emergency fund should be easily accessible when needed.
q.ef_4_exp: Liquidity is key: a fund you can't access for weeks is useless in a true emergency. You need the money quickly when crisis strikes.
q.ef_5: Which of these options is best for storing an emergency fund?
q.ef_5_c: Liquid and safe instrument: high-yield savings or money market fund
q.ef_5_exp: An emergency fund combines liquidity and safety. High-yield savings accounts and money market funds are ideal: accessible within hours and with some return.
q.svc_1: What distinguishes compound interest from simple interest?
q.svc_1_a: Compound interest generates returns on capital plus previously earned interest
q.svc_1_b: Simple interest generates more money over the long term
q.svc_1_d: Compound interest only exists in cryptocurrencies
q.svc_1_exp: Compound interest reinvests earned interest, generating interest on interest. This creates a powerful 'snowball' effect that grows enormously over time.
q.svc_2: Over the long term, compound interest generates significantly more wealth than simple interest.
q.svc_2_exp: With $1,000 at 10% annually: simple interest gives $3,000 in 20 years. Compound interest gives $6,727. The difference becomes huge as time passes.
q.svc_3: With $1,000 at 10% simple annual interest, how much do you earn in 2 years?
q.svc_3_exp: Simple interest: $1,000 × 10% × 2 years = $200 total gain. The capital does not generate interest on accumulated interest.
q.svc_4: The phrase 'Compound interest is the eighth wonder of the world' is attributed to Einstein.
q.svc_4_exp: Although not historically confirmed, the phrase captures the powerful multiplying effect of compound interest that would impress any mathematician.
q.svc_5: How often can compound interest be applied?
q.svc_5_d: It can be daily, monthly, quarterly, or annual
q.svc_5_exp: Compound interest can be calculated at different intervals. The more frequent the compounding (daily vs annual), the greater the capitalization effect.
q.r72_1: What is the Rule of 72 used for?
q.r72_1_a: Estimating how many years it takes to double an investment (or a debt)
q.r72_1_d: Converting currencies at the official exchange rate
q.r72_1_exp: The Rule of 72 is simple: divide 72 by the annual interest rate to get the approximate years needed to double your money. Also works to see how fast inflation erodes savings.
q.r72_2: With a 6% annual rate, the Rule of 72 says money doubles in approximately 12 years.
q.r72_2_exp: 72 ÷ 6% = 12 years. It's a very useful approximation to intuitively understand the power of time and interest rate on an investment.
q.r72_3: If inflation is 36% annually, how many years does it take to cut purchasing power in half?
q.r72_3_exp: 72 ÷ 36% = 2 years. With 36% inflation, your money would be worth half in just 2 years if you don't protect it. Same compound effect, but working against you.
q.r72_4: The Rule of 72 gives mathematically exact results in all cases.
q.r72_4_exp: It's an excellent approximation, not an exact calculation. It works very well for rates between 6% and 10%. For very high rates (like high inflation) it underestimates the actual time.
q.r72_5: How many years does it take for an investment at 9% annually to double, per the Rule of 72?
q.r72_5_exp: 72 ÷ 9% = 8 years. If you invest $10,000 today at 9% compound annually, in 8 years you'd have approximately $20,000.
q.was_1: What is a stock in financial terms?
q.was_1_a: A fraction of ownership in a company
q.was_1_b: A loan you make to a company
q.was_1_exp: Buying a stock makes you a shareholder (part-owner) of that company. You own a small piece and are entitled to a share of its profits.
q.was_2: When you buy shares of a company, you become one of its owners.
q.was_2_exp: Exactly. A shareholder owns a portion of the company, proportional to the number of shares held out of the total shares issued.
q.was_3: Where are Argentine stocks bought and sold?
q.was_3_exp: ByMA is Argentina's main stock exchange. Stocks, bonds, options, and other financial instruments are traded there.
q.was_4: Stocks always guarantee a profit to the investor.
q.was_4_exp: Stocks are risk assets. Their price can go up or down based on company performance and market conditions. They can even lose all value if the company goes bankrupt.
q.was_5: What determines the price of a stock in the market?
q.was_5_a: What buyers and sellers are willing to pay and receive
q.was_5_b: The government sets an official price for each stock
q.was_5_c: The manufacturing cost of the company's products
q.was_5_d: The size of the company's main building
q.was_5_exp: Price is determined by supply and demand. If more people want to buy than sell, the price rises. If everyone wants to sell, the price falls.
q.htb_1: What do you need to buy stocks in Argentina?
q.htb_1_a: Open a trading account at an ALyC authorized by the CNV
q.htb_1_c: Get special authorization from the BCRA
q.htb_1_d: Be a bank or financial institution employee
q.htb_1_exp: An ALyC (Settlement and Clearing Agent) is the entity authorized by the CNV to operate in the capital market. You can open an account from your phone on platforms like IOL, Balanz or PPI.
q.htb_2: An ALyC is a Settlement and Clearing Agent authorized by the CNV to operate in the capital market.
q.htb_2_exp: ALyCs like InvertirOnline, Balanz, PPI, Cocos Capital, and others are regulated platforms where you can buy and sell stocks, bonds, and funds in Argentina.
q.htb_3: What does placing a 'market order' mean when buying stocks?
q.htb_3_a: Waiting until the price drops to the desired level
q.htb_3_b: Buying at the best available price at that moment
q.htb_3_c: Buying with a special discount from the broker
q.htb_3_exp: A market order executes immediately at the available price. It's the simplest way to buy. Alternative: a limit order, where you set the maximum price you're willing to pay.
q.htb_4: Only Argentine residents can invest in stocks listed on the ByMA.
q.htb_4_exp: Foreigners can also invest in the ByMA by opening an account at a local ALyC. Argentine residency is not required to operate in the capital market.
q.htb_5: What does 'diversifying' an investment portfolio mean?
q.htb_5_a: Putting all capital into the most well-known company in the market
q.htb_5_c: Selling everything when the market starts to fall
q.htb_5_d: Distributing money across different assets to reduce total risk
q.htb_5_exp: Diversifying means not putting all your eggs in one basket. If one investment falls, others can offset the loss. It reduces risk without necessarily reducing returns.
q.div_1_a: The distribution of part of a company's profits to its shareholders
q.div_1_b: The current price of a stock in the market
q.div_1_c: The interest paid by a government bond
q.div_1_d: The commissions charged by the broker per transaction
q.div_1_exp: When a company has profits, it can distribute them to shareholders as dividends. It's a form of passive income — you get paid just for owning part of the company.
q.div_2: All companies listed on the stock exchange are required to pay dividends to shareholders.
q.div_2_exp: Each company decides whether to pay dividends or not. Many reinvest profits to grow (Amazon went years without paying them). The board of directors votes on this decision.
q.div_3: How often are dividends typically paid?
q.div_3_b: Only once in the company's lifetime
q.div_3_c: Quarterly or annually, depending on the company
q.div_3_exp: Most companies pay dividends quarterly or annually. Some pay semi-annually. Weekly or daily payments are practically non-existent.
q.div_4: Reinvesting received dividends leverages the compound interest effect.
q.div_4_exp: If you use dividends to buy more shares, those new shares will also generate dividends. Over time, this cycle can be extremely powerful for wealth building.
q.div_5_a: The current market price of the stock
q.div_5_b: The company's total net profit for the year
q.div_5_c: The annual dividend as a percentage of the stock's current price
q.div_5_d: The total number of shares issued by the company
q.div_5_exp: Dividend yield = (annual dividend / stock price) × 100. If a stock is worth $100 and pays $5 in dividends, the yield is 5%.
q.wic_1_a: A decentralized digital currency that uses cryptography to secure transactions
q.wic_1_b: A digital bill issued by the central bank
q.wic_1_c: A share of technology companies like Apple or Google
q.wic_1_d: A type of digital government bond
q.wic_1_exp: Cryptocurrencies are digital currencies that use cryptography and blockchain technology to function without needing a central bank or government to control them.
q.wic_2: Bitcoin is controlled by a central bank or government.
q.wic_2_exp: Bitcoin is completely decentralized. No bank, government, or company controls it. It runs through consensus among its global network of users.
q.wic_3: In what year was Bitcoin created?
q.wic_3_exp: Bitcoin was created in 2009 by a person or group using the pseudonym 'Satoshi Nakamoto', publishing the famous whitepaper amid the 2008 global financial crisis.
q.wic_4: Cryptocurrencies can be very volatile, losing or gaining a lot of value in a short time.
q.wic_4_exp: High volatility is a central feature of the crypto market. Bitcoin fell over 80% in 2022 and surged over 500% in other cycles. The risk is very real!
q.wic_5_a: A highly speculative and volatile cryptocurrency
q.wic_5_b: A digital share of a technology company
q.wic_5_c: A cryptocurrency whose value is pegged to a stable currency like the US dollar
q.wic_5_exp: Stablecoins (USDT, USDC, DAI) maintain their value tied to the dollar. They're very popular for digitally dollar-protecting savings without traditional banking restrictions.
q.bc_1_a: A distributed database of chained blocks, public and immutable
q.bc_1_b: A fully digital bank without branches
q.bc_1_exp: A blockchain is a chain of blocks where each block contains transactions. It's distributed (no central server) and immutable (practically impossible to alter).
q.bc_2: Bitcoin's blockchain is public and anyone can verify its transactions.
q.bc_2_exp: You can view all Bitcoin transactions on explorers like blockchain.com. This total transparency is one of blockchain's big advantages over traditional banking.
q.bc_3: What makes blockchain very difficult to modify or hack?
q.bc_3_a: It's stored on a single highly secure server
q.bc_3_b: Each country's government actively protects it
q.bc_3_c: Its chained structure and distributed consensus among thousands of nodes
q.bc_3_d: It uses extremely long and complex passwords
q.bc_3_exp: To alter one block, you'd have to change all following blocks and convince more than 50% of the network. With thousands of distributed nodes, this is practically impossible.
q.bc_4_exp: Ethereum, Solana, Cardano, and thousands of other cryptocurrencies also use blockchain. Even sectors like healthcare, logistics, and digital art (NFTs) are adopting it.
q.bc_5: What are 'nodes' in a blockchain network?
q.bc_5_a: The coins or tokens circulating within the blockchain
q.bc_5_b: Pending transactions waiting to be confirmed
q.bc_5_d: The computers that maintain and validate the network
q.bc_5_exp: Nodes are computers around the world that store a complete copy of the blockchain and validate each new transaction according to the protocol's rules.
q.priceb_1: What mainly determines the price of a good in a market economy?
q.priceb_1_a: Supply and demand for the good
q.priceb_1_exp: In a market economy, prices arise from the balance between how much is available (supply) and how much consumers want to buy (demand).
q.priceb_2: When there is more money circulating in the economy than goods available, prices tend to rise.
q.priceb_2_exp: This is the core of inflation: too much money chasing too few goods. It's one of the classic causes of price increases.
q.priceb_3: What does it mean when economists say there is 'price distortion' in an economy?
q.priceb_3_b: Inflation has been zero for a long time
q.priceb_3_c: Prices don't reflect real supply and demand due to controls or distortions
q.priceb_3_d: All goods have the same price
q.priceb_3_exp: Price distortions occur when the government sets price controls, subsidies, or regulations that prevent prices from adjusting naturally to supply and demand.
q.priceb_4: In Argentina, the government has historically applied price controls to try to contain inflation.
q.priceb_4_exp: Argentina has had recurring price control programs (Precios Cuidados, Maximum Prices, etc.). These measures usually generate shortages and parallel markets.
q.priceb_5: Why can a good suddenly become more expensive even if demand hasn't changed?
q.priceb_5_a: Because buyers decided to pay more
q.priceb_5_b: Because the government raised the tax rate
q.priceb_5_c: Because something happened with the dollar
q.priceb_5_d: Because its supply was reduced (production cost rose, shortages, etc.)
q.priceb_5_exp: If supply falls — for example, due to a drought that destroys a harvest — the price rises even without increased demand. Less good + same demand = higher price.
q.iet_1_a: Total income before taxes and deductions
q.iet_1_b: What you actually receive after all deductions
q.iet_1_exp: Net income is what actually reaches your pocket: gross income minus taxes, contributions, and any other deduction. It's the amount you can actually use.
q.iet_2: If your expenses are greater than your income for several consecutive months, you will be depleting your savings or going into debt.
q.iet_2_exp: A negative financial balance means you spend more than you earn. If you have no savings buffer, you end up going into debt — which worsens the financial situation over time.
q.iet_3: What concept describes the idea that money today is worth more than the same amount in the future?
q.iet_3_exp: The time value of money tells us that $100 today has more purchasing power than $100 in a year, because today you can invest it, spend it, or protect it from inflation.
q.iet_4: In Argentina, having a fixed peso salary for several months is equivalent to a real pay cut.
q.iet_4_exp: With annual inflation above 100%, a salary that doesn't adjust loses purchasing power month by month. Earning the same pesos means buying fewer goods.
q.iet_5: Which of the following best describes 'variable income'?
q.iet_5_a: A fixed monthly salary from employment
q.iet_5_b: A bank term deposit with a fixed rate
q.iet_5_c: Income that changes based on sales, commissions, or freelance work
q.iet_5_exp: Variable income doesn't have a fixed amount: it depends on performance, sales, clients, or hours worked. Examples: freelancers, salespeople on commission, or entrepreneurs.
q.mi_1: What is the CPI (Consumer Price Index)?
q.mi_1_a: An index that measures the exchange rate variation
q.mi_1_b: A basket of goods and services that tracks price changes over time
q.mi_1_c: The interest rate set by the central bank
q.mi_1_exp: The CPI measures price changes of a representative basket of goods and services consumed by households. It's the main instrument for calculating official inflation.
q.mi_2: In Argentina, INDEC is the official body responsible for measuring inflation.
q.mi_2_exp: INDEC (National Institute of Statistics and Census) publishes the CPI monthly. Its credibility has been questioned in various political periods, generating private measurement alternatives.
q.mi_3: If the CPI went from 100 to 135 in one year, what was the annual inflation rate?
q.mi_3_exp: Inflation = (new - old) / old × 100 = (135-100)/100 × 100 = 35%. A rise from 100 to 135 represents 35% inflation.
q.mi_4: The 'core inflation' excludes volatile items like food and energy to show the underlying trend.
q.mi_4_exp: Core inflation removes seasonal goods (like vegetables) and regulated prices (like fuel) to show the more stable part of inflation. It's useful for monetary policy.
q.mi_5_a: Prices rose 5% compared to the same month last year
q.mi_5_b: Prices rose 5% compared to the previous month
q.mi_5_d: The central bank raised the rate by 5 percentage points
q.mi_5_exp: Monthly inflation compares prices from one month to the previous one. 5% monthly means that what cost $100 in January costs $105 in February.
q.efi_1: What is the main effect of high inflation on people's purchasing power?
q.efi_1_a: It increases because they have more pesos
q.efi_1_b: It decreases because the same pesos buy fewer things
q.efi_1_c: It stays the same as long as salaries rise
q.efi_1_exp: Inflation erodes purchasing power: even if you have the same amount of money, it buys less. People who can't adjust their income quickly are the hardest hit.
q.efi_2: Inflation tends to benefit debtors in fixed nominal terms, because they pay back with money worth less.
q.efi_2_exp: If you took a loan at a fixed rate and inflation shoots up, the real value of what you owe decreases. This is why in inflationary periods, going into fixed-rate debt can be advantageous.
q.efi_3: What happens to savings in a bank account if the interest rate is lower than inflation?
q.efi_3_c: They lose real value, even though the nominal balance grows
q.efi_3_d: They are automatically protected by the central bank
q.efi_3_exp: If inflation is 100% and the savings rate is 60%, your pesos increase in number, but buy much less. In real terms, you're losing money by keeping it in the bank.
q.efi_4: High inflation redistributes income from savers to debtors and from the poor to those with real assets.
q.efi_4_exp: People with real assets (land, stocks, foreign currency) are protected. The poor, who hold everything in cash, lose the most with each inflationary spike.
q.efi_5: Which sector usually benefits most during a high inflation period?
q.efi_5_b: People with variable rate savings in pesos
q.efi_5_c: Holders of real assets like real estate or stocks
q.efi_5_d: Government employees with delayed salary adjustments
q.efi_5_exp: Real assets hold value when money loses it. That's why during inflationary crises, real estate, stocks, and hard currencies become preferred refuges.
q.hyp_1: What level of monthly inflation is typically considered 'hyperinflation'?
q.hyp_1_exp: The classic definition (Cagan, 1956) sets hyperinflation at 50% or more per month. Argentina in 1989 reached 3,079% annual inflation — a real hyperinflationary episode.
q.hyp_2: Argentina experienced a hyperinflationary episode in 1989 during the Alfonsín government.
q.hyp_2_exp: In 1989, Argentina lived through the only documented episode of hyperinflation in its history, with monthly rates exceeding 50%. It led to early handover of power to Menem.
q.hyp_3_c: Excessive money printing without backing, often combined with a fiscal crisis
q.hyp_3_exp: Hyperinflation usually starts when a government prints money massively to finance its spending, losing all monetary credibility. The self-fulfilling expectation of inflation further accelerates the collapse.
q.hyp_4: Hyperinflation can destroy a country's entire savings system in a matter of weeks.
q.hyp_4_exp: During hyperinflation, money loses value so fast that people rush to exchange it for real goods. Banks collapse, credit disappears, and the entire economic system is disrupted.
q.hyp_5: Which country experienced the most extreme hyperinflation in modern history?
q.hyp_5_exp: Zimbabwe in 2008 reached an estimated 89.7 sextillion percent annual inflation. Germany's 1923 episode and Venezuela's 2018 crisis are also extreme historical cases.
q.sdl_1: According to the law of supply and demand, what happens to the price of a good when its supply falls?
q.sdl_1_d: The good disappears from the market
q.sdl_1_exp: Less supply + same demand = upward pressure on prices. Producers can charge more because the good is scarcer. Classic example: drought → less grain → higher flour price.
q.sdl_2: When the price of a good rises, buyers typically demand less of that good.
q.sdl_2_exp: This is the law of demand: as price rises, quantity demanded falls, all else equal. Consumers substitute for cheaper alternatives or simply stop buying.
q.sdl_3_a: All goods have the same price
q.sdl_3_c: The price at which quantity supplied equals quantity demanded
q.sdl_3_exp: Equilibrium is the price and quantity where what producers want to supply matches what consumers want to buy. Above equilibrium there's surplus; below, there's shortage.
q.sdl_4: Price controls below equilibrium tend to generate shortages of the controlled good.
q.sdl_4_exp: If the state sets a price below equilibrium, producers offer less (not profitable) and consumers want more (cheap). The result: shelves empty. Classic in Argentina: price controls on food.
q.sdl_5: What usually happens to the price of umbrellas when it starts raining a lot?
q.sdl_5_b: It stays constant regardless of the weather
q.sdl_5_c: It falls because the government subsidizes them
q.sdl_5_d: It rises because demand increases with the same supply
q.sdl_5_exp: Rain increases the demand for umbrellas without immediately changing supply. The equilibrium price rises. This is supply and demand working in real time.
q.mkt_1: What is a 'monopoly' in market terms?
q.mkt_1_a: A market with many competing sellers
q.mkt_1_c: A market with a single seller that controls price and quantity
q.mkt_1_d: A market where prices are fixed by the government
q.mkt_1_exp: In a monopoly, one seller dominates the entire market. Without competition, it can set higher prices and offer less quantity than in a competitive market.
q.mkt_2: In a perfectly competitive market, no individual buyer or seller can influence the price.
q.mkt_2_exp: Perfect competition implies many sellers with identical products and perfect information. Each one accepts the market price. In practice it barely exists, but it's the theoretical reference.
q.mkt_3: What distinguishes an oligopoly from perfect competition?
q.mkt_3_a: In an oligopoly there are many small sellers
q.mkt_3_b: In an oligopoly there are no products to sell
q.mkt_3_c: In an oligopoly a few large firms dominate and can influence prices
q.mkt_3_d: In an oligopoly prices are set by the state
q.mkt_3_exp: An oligopoly has a few dominant firms (e.g., the major Argentine telecom companies). They can coordinate prices (tacitly or explicitly), which is harmful to competition.
q.mkt_4: A monopoly tends to produce more and charge less than would occur in a competitive market.
q.mkt_4_exp: It's the opposite: monopolies maximize their profit by restricting production and raising prices. Without competition, there's no incentive to lower prices or innovate.
q.mkt_5: Which of the following is an example of a natural monopoly?
q.mkt_5_b: A gas distribution network or electricity transmission
q.mkt_5_d: A tech startup with a new app
q.mkt_5_exp: Natural monopolies arise where having multiple competitors would be inefficient (e.g., duplicating gas pipelines). Governments regulate them to avoid abuse.
q.ela_1: What does the concept of 'price elasticity of demand' measure?
q.ela_1_a: How much the price of a good changes over time
q.ela_1_b: How sensitive the quantity demanded is to a change in price
q.ela_1_c: The speed at which a company can change its prices
q.ela_1_d: The gap between formal and informal prices
q.ela_1_exp: Price elasticity measures how much quantity demanded changes when price changes. If demand changes a lot with a small price change, it's elastic; if it barely changes, it's inelastic.
q.ela_2: A product with inelastic demand will see very little change in sales even if its price rises significantly.
q.ela_2_exp: Inelastic goods are necessities with few substitutes: medications, fuel, basic food. People keep buying them even at higher prices because they have no alternative.
q.ela_3: Which of the following goods typically has the most inelastic demand?
q.ela_3_exp: Insulin is a vital medication with no substitute for those who need it. Its demand is highly inelastic: people buy it regardless of price because their life depends on it.
q.ela_4: Products with many available substitutes tend to have more elastic demand.
q.ela_4_exp: If a brand of mineral water raises its price, buyers switch to another brand. The existence of substitutes makes demand elastic: small price change → large change in quantity.
q.ela_5: Why is elasticity important for a company setting its prices?
q.ela_5_a: It defines the tax rate the company must pay
q.ela_5_b: It allows estimating how quantity sold will change when changing the price
q.ela_5_c: It sets the level of competition in the market
q.ela_5_d: It determines the cost of raw materials
q.ela_5_exp: If demand is inelastic, raising the price increases revenue. If elastic, raising the price loses many customers and revenue falls. Knowing elasticity is key to pricing strategy.
q.gdp_1: What does GDP (Gross Domestic Product) measure?
q.gdp_1_a: The total savings of the population
q.gdp_1_b: The total value of all goods and services produced in a country in a period
q.gdp_1_c: The total amount of money in circulation
q.gdp_1_exp: GDP is the broadest measure of economic activity. It adds up the final value of everything produced (cars, food, services, construction) within a country in a given period.
q.gdp_2: A country can have a growing GDP and at the same time have a deteriorating standard of living for the average citizen.
q.gdp_2_exp: GDP grows on average, but if inequality is high, most of the growth can concentrate in a small rich sector. GDP per capita and income distribution matter more for real well-being.
q.gdp_3: What does 'real GDP' mean, as opposed to nominal GDP?
q.gdp_3_a: GDP expressed in dollars instead of pesos
q.gdp_3_b: GDP that includes underground or informal economy
q.gdp_3_c: GDP adjusted for inflation to reflect genuine productive growth
q.gdp_3_exp: Nominal GDP rises with inflation even if production doesn't grow. Real GDP removes the price effect to show if the economy actually produced more real goods and services.
q.gdp_4: Argentina's GDP per capita is one of the highest in Latin America.
q.gdp_4_exp: Although historically Argentina had a high GDP per capita for the region, recurrent crises have eroded it. Today it is intermediate compared to Chile, Uruguay, or Brazil.
q.gdp_5: What happens to a country's GDP during an economic recession?
q.gdp_5_a: It always grows, but more slowly
q.gdp_5_b: It falls for two or more consecutive quarters
q.gdp_5_c: It stays stable regardless of economic conditions
q.gdp_5_d: It doubles because of higher government spending
q.gdp_5_exp: By conventional definition, a recession is two or more consecutive quarters of GDP decline. Production falls, unemployment rises, and consumption contracts.
q.emp_1: What does the unemployment rate measure?
q.emp_1_a: The percentage of the total population without work
q.emp_1_b: The percentage of the economically active population that is looking for work and can't find it
q.emp_1_c: The number of people who don't want to work
q.emp_1_d: The total number of jobs created each month
q.emp_1_exp: The unemployment rate only counts those actively seeking employment. It excludes the inactive (retirees, students, discouraged workers). It measures pressure on the labor market.
q.emp_2: 'Structural unemployment' is the one that arises because of a mismatch between the skills workers have and what the market demands.
q.emp_2_exp: Structural unemployment is more persistent than cyclical. It occurs when an industry disappears (e.g., factories replaced by automation) and workers lack skills for new available jobs.
q.emp_3: What type of unemployment is generated when a worker voluntarily quits their job to look for a better one?
q.emp_3_exp: Frictional unemployment is normal and even healthy: it's the transition time between jobs. It exists even in full employment economies. It's not a major policy problem.
q.emp_4: Informal employment in Argentina represents more than 40% of total workers.
q.emp_4_exp: Argentina has a high informality rate. Informal workers lack social coverage, cannot access unemployment insurance, and are very vulnerable in times of crisis.
q.emp_5: Which of the following is considered 'full employment' in economic terms?
q.emp_5_a: 100% of the population has formal employment
q.emp_5_c: A low and stable unemployment rate, with only frictional unemployment
q.emp_5_exp: Full employment doesn't mean 0% unemployment (that's impossible). It means only frictional unemployment remains — that is, only those switching jobs. There's no structural or cyclical unemployment.
q.pov_1: What is the difference between poverty and indigence?
q.pov_1_a: There is no difference, they are the same
q.pov_1_b: Poverty measures access to housing; indigence to food
q.pov_1_c: Indigence is a more severe level: people can't even cover their basic food basket
q.pov_1_d: Poverty applies to countries; indigence to individuals
q.pov_1_exp: In Argentina's official measurement: poverty = cannot cover the total basic basket (food + other goods and services); indigence = cannot cover even the food basket alone.
q.pov_2: Poverty in Argentina is measured based on the ability to access a basic basket of goods and services.
q.pov_2_exp: INDEC measures poverty by comparing household income with the Total Basic Basket (CBT) value. If the income doesn't reach the CBT, the household is considered poor.
q.pov_3_a: An index measuring the country's annual economic growth
q.pov_3_b: The official inflation measured by INDEC
q.pov_3_c: An index that measures income inequality distribution in a society
q.pov_3_d: The percentage of people below the poverty line
q.pov_3_exp: The Gini coefficient goes from 0 (perfect equality) to 1 (one person has everything). Argentina has a Gini of around 0.42-0.45, indicating significant inequality.
q.pov_4: Economic growth automatically reduces poverty levels in a country.
q.pov_4_exp: Growth can reduce poverty, but not automatically. It depends on how growth is distributed, what happens to employment, and whether social policies accompany it. There are cases of growth without poverty reduction.
q.pov_5: Which of the following factors contributes most to reducing poverty in the long term?
q.pov_5_c: Access to quality education and formal employment
q.pov_5_exp: Education and formal employment are the most sustainable routes out of poverty. Transfers help in the short term, but the structural path is through human capital and stable jobs.
q.tbl_1: What does the trade balance measure?
q.tbl_1_a: The total amount of dollars in the central bank
q.tbl_1_b: The difference between a country's exports and imports
q.tbl_1_d: The exchange rate between the peso and the dollar
q.tbl_1_exp: The trade balance = exports - imports. If exports > imports, there is a trade surplus. If imports > exports, there is a deficit. It's key for understanding foreign currency availability.
q.tbl_2: A trade surplus means a country imports more than it exports.
q.tbl_2_exp: It's the opposite: surplus = exports > imports. The country receives more foreign currency than it spends. Deficit = imports > exports, meaning it 'bleeds' dollars.
q.tbl_3: Why is the trade balance especially important for Argentina?
q.tbl_3_a: Because it determines the official dollar exchange rate
q.tbl_3_b: Because it's the main indicator of inflation
q.tbl_3_c: Because Argentina depends on dollars to import fuel, technology, and pay its debt
q.tbl_3_d: Because the IMF measures it to approve loans
q.tbl_3_exp: Argentina earns dollars mainly from agri-food exports (soybeans, corn, wheat). Without enough trade surplus, there are no dollars to pay debts or import what the economy needs.
q.tbl_4: Devaluing the local currency tends to make exports cheaper and imports more expensive.
q.tbl_4_exp: A devaluation makes local products cheaper in foreign currency (more competitive abroad) and imported goods more expensive in pesos. It tends to improve the trade balance, but at the cost of domestic inflation.
q.tbl_5: Which sector generates the most export dollars for Argentina?
q.tbl_5_exp: Argentina is a global agri-food power. Soybean complex, cereals, and beef generate the bulk of export dollars. This concentration makes the economy vulnerable to droughts and international prices.
q.monp_1: What is the main role of the central bank in monetary policy?
q.monp_1_a: Setting the official exchange rate for all imports
q.monp_1_b: Regulating the money supply and interest rates to maintain monetary stability
q.monp_1_c: Collecting taxes and financing the public deficit
q.monp_1_exp: The central bank controls the money supply and short-term interest rates. Its goal is usually price stability (inflation control) and sometimes also supporting economic growth and employment.
q.monp_2: When the central bank raises interest rates, it tends to reduce inflation by making credit more expensive.
q.monp_2_exp: Higher rates make loans more expensive, discouraging consumption and investment. Less spending = less demand pressure on prices. It's the classic transmission mechanism of monetary policy.
q.monp_3: What tool does the BCRA (Central Bank of Argentina) use to absorb excess pesos from the market?
q.monp_3_c: Selling dollars in the official market
q.monp_3_d: Issuing Leliqs (Liquidity Notes) or passes to banks
q.monp_3_exp: Leliqs and repo operations are instruments the BCRA uses to 'sterilize' pesos: banks park their excess pesos in these instruments in exchange for a high interest rate, removing them from circulation.
q.monp_4: Printing more money is always an effective way to stimulate economic growth.
q.monp_4_exp: In the short term printing can reactivate, but if there's no real productive growth, all it does is generate inflation. Argentina has proven this many times: excessive emission ends in price explosion.
q.monp_5_b: Replacing pesos with dollars in the economy
q.monp_5_c: The central bank absorbing excess liquidity to prevent it from causing inflation
q.monp_5_d: Printing money to finance state spending
q.monp_5_exp: Sterilization is when the central bank absorbs the pesos it previously emitted (e.g., buying dollars) to prevent them from generating inflation. It's done through bonds, passes, etc.
q.fisp_1_a: The regulation of exchange rates by the central bank
q.fisp_1_b: The monetary policy applied to reduce inflation
q.fisp_1_c: The set of decisions about government spending and taxes to influence the economy
q.fisp_1_d: The trade policy applied to balance exports and imports
q.fisp_1_exp: Fiscal policy is the government's tool to manage the economy through its spending and taxes. It can be expansive (more spending, fewer taxes) or contractionary (less spending, more taxes).
q.fisp_2: A government running a fiscal deficit is spending more than it collects in taxes.
q.fisp_2_exp: Deficit = expenses > revenues. To finance it, the government borrows (debt) or prints money. Both alternatives can generate problems: more debt or more inflation.
q.fisp_3: What does it mean that a government has a 'primary fiscal surplus'?
q.fisp_3_b: It has more exports than imports
q.fisp_3_c: Its revenues exceed its primary expenditures, before counting interest on debt
q.fisp_3_d: Its GDP grew for three consecutive quarters
q.fisp_3_exp: The primary surplus excludes debt interest payments. It shows if the government 'pays for itself' with current revenues. It's key to assessing fiscal sustainability.
q.fisp_4: High fiscal deficits tend to generate inflationary pressures when financed with money printing.
q.fisp_4_exp: If the government can't borrow and prints pesos to cover its deficit, more money circulates without more production. The result: inflation. This is one of Argentina's recurring problems.
q.fisp_5: Which of the following is an example of expansionary fiscal policy?
q.fisp_5_exp: Expansionary fiscal policy injects resources into the economy: more spending or less taxes. It stimulates demand and activity but can worsen the fiscal deficit.
q.ait_1: What is an interest rate hike by the central bank intended to do?
q.ait_1_a: Stimulate bank lending to grow the economy
q.ait_1_b: Reduce the exchange rate and strengthen the currency
q.ait_1_c: Cool inflation by making borrowing more expensive and discouraging consumption
q.ait_1_exp: When borrowing is expensive, people and companies take fewer loans and spend less. Lower demand = less pressure on prices. It's the classic rate mechanism to fight inflation.
q.ait_2: A contractionary fiscal policy (spending cuts) can help reduce inflationary pressure.
q.ait_2_exp: Less government spending = less money injected into the economy = less demand. It reduces inflationary pressure but can slow activity and increase unemployment in the short term.
q.ait_3: What is 'indexation' in the economic context?
q.ait_3_b: Linking wages, pensions, rents, or contracts to inflation to automatically maintain their real value
q.ait_3_exp: Indexation ties values to a price index. It protects against inflation but can become self-fulfilling: if everyone adjusts to past inflation, it perpetuates it. Classic dilemma in Argentina.
q.ait_4: Price controls are an effective and sustainable long-term measure to reduce inflation.
q.ait_4_exp: Price controls can temporarily contain prices, but they don't address the root cause (excess money or high costs). In the medium term they generate shortages, parallel markets, and eventually worse inflation.
q.ait_5: What was Argentina's 'Convertibility' and how did it attempt to contain inflation?
q.ait_5_a: It was a program of price controls on basic goods
q.ait_5_b: It was an exchange control that limited dollar purchases
q.ait_5_c: It fixed the peso to the dollar 1:1, preventing the BCRA from printing freely
q.ait_5_d: It was a fiscal reform that eliminated state subsidies
q.ait_5_exp: Convertibility (1991-2001) fixed 1 peso = 1 dollar and tied the BCRA's emission to its dollar reserves. It stopped hyperinflation but ended in the 2001 crisis.
q.exr_1: What is the nominal exchange rate?
q.exr_1_a: The price of a barrel of oil in dollars
q.exr_1_b: The price of one currency expressed in another currency
q.exr_1_c: The interest rate paid by Argentine government bonds
q.exr_1_d: The difference between the price index in two countries
q.exr_1_exp: The nominal exchange rate is how many units of one currency you need to get one of another. Example: if $1 USD = $900 ARS, that's the nominal exchange rate.
q.exr_2: In Argentina the only official dollar rate is the one set by the BCRA.
q.exr_2_exp: Argentina has had multiple exchange rate schemes simultaneously: official, blue (black market), MEP, cash-with-settlement, savings dollar, etc. This complexity distorts economic decisions.
q.exr_3_a: A reduction in the interest rate set by the central bank
q.exr_3_b: When the government cuts public spending to reduce the deficit
q.exr_3_c: The loss of formal value of the local currency against foreign currencies
q.exr_3_d: When inflation drops sharply in a very short period
q.exr_3_exp: Devaluation is when the local currency loses value against others. In Argentina, each official devaluation (like in January 2002 or December 2023) instantly raised the price of imported goods.
q.exr_4: A depreciation of the local currency makes a country's exports more competitive internationally.
q.exr_4_exp: If the peso depreciates, Argentine products become cheaper in dollars. A foreigner can buy more with the same money. This tends to boost exports, especially agriculture.
q.exr_5_a: The unofficial exchange rate in the informal market
q.exr_5_b: The nominal exchange rate adjusted for the inflation differential between the two countries
q.exr_5_c: The exchange rate set by the IMF
q.exr_5_d: The rate applied to formal imports
q.exr_5_exp: The real exchange rate = nominal rate adjusted for relative prices. Even if the nominal rate rises, if domestic inflation is higher, the real rate can be falling (Argentina loses competitiveness).
q.intr_1_a: The gold stored in the presidential palace
q.intr_1_b: The foreign currency, gold, and other liquid assets held by the central bank
q.intr_1_c: The total public debt in dollars
q.intr_1_exp: International reserves are the hard assets held by the central bank: dollars, gold, SDRs, etc. They serve to support the currency, pay external commitments, and intervene in the exchange market.
q.intr_2: Low international reserves reduce a country's ability to defend its currency and pay its external debts.
q.intr_2_exp: Without reserves, the central bank can't intervene to contain the dollar or guarantee imports. It's a central vulnerability of the Argentine economy that has triggered multiple exchange crises.
q.intr_3: What are 'net reserves' of the BCRA?
q.intr_3_c: Gross reserves minus external liabilities (swap lines, repo obligations, etc.)
q.intr_3_d: The reserves set aside to pay for imports
q.intr_3_exp: Net reserves deduct from gross reserves what the BCRA 'owes' (swaps, repos, etc.). They reflect the effective margin of maneuver. Argentina has had net reserves in negative territory.
q.intr_4: A country with high international reserves is more vulnerable to currency attacks.
q.intr_4_exp: It's the opposite: high reserves signal that the central bank can defend the exchange rate. Low reserves invite speculative attacks — everyone rushes to buy dollars before a devaluation.
q.intr_5: Why does Argentina accumulate reserves primarily through agricultural exports?
q.intr_5_a: Because agriculture is the most technologically advanced sector
q.intr_5_b: Because the soy and grain complex generates the most dollar income from exports
q.intr_5_c: Because the IMF requires it as a loan condition
q.intr_5_d: Because agricultural products have no import costs
q.intr_5_exp: The soybean complex (oil, pellets, beans), corn, and wheat are Argentina's main hard currency generators. That's why droughts or export restrictions have such a direct impact on BCRA reserves.
q.ftr_1_b: Interest paid by companies for loans from the state
q.ftr_1_c: A percentage of the export value that the exporter must give to the state
q.ftr_1_d: Currency controls applied to travel abroad
q.ftr_1_exp: Retenciones (or derechos de exportación) are taxes on exports. The main ones apply to soy, corn, and wheat. They generate fiscal revenue and reduce international prices for domestic consumption.
q.ftr_2: Free trade between countries always benefits all parties involved equally.
q.ftr_2_exp: Trade generates aggregate gains, but they are not distributed equally. Some domestic sectors can be hurt (e.g., local industry competing with cheap imports). That's why trade policy is politically sensitive.
q.ftr_3: What is the purpose of a trade tariff?
q.ftr_3_a: To encourage imports by reducing their price
q.ftr_3_b: To protect local production by making imported goods more expensive
q.ftr_3_exp: A tariff is a tax on imports. It raises the price of foreign goods, making domestic production more competitive. The tradeoff: it protects jobs but can raise prices for consumers.
q.ftr_4: Argentina's membership in Mercosur means it has a common external tariff for imports from outside the bloc.
q.ftr_4_exp: Yes, Mercosur has a Common External Tariff (CET) applied to products from outside the bloc. Argentina, Brazil, Uruguay, and Paraguay coordinate these tariffs, though with exceptions and frequent disputes.
q.ftr_5: What is 'comparative advantage' in international trade?
q.ftr_5_a: Selling products more expensive than competitors
q.ftr_5_c: Specializing in producing what you do at a relatively lower cost than other countries
q.ftr_5_d: Importing everything possible to keep domestic prices low
q.ftr_5_exp: Comparative advantage (Ricardo) says each country should specialize in what it produces relatively most efficiently. Even if one country is better at everything, trade still benefits both parties.
q.cyc_1: What defines a 'business cycle' in economics?
q.cyc_1_a: The seasonal variation in agricultural prices
q.cyc_1_b: The recurring fluctuations of economic activity between expansion and contraction
q.cyc_1_d: The annual change in the exchange rate
q.cyc_1_exp: The economic cycle describes how the economy expands (growth, employment, investment) and then contracts (recession, unemployment). These fluctuations are natural and recurring.
q.cyc_2: During an economic recession, unemployment typically rises.
q.cyc_2_exp: In recessions, companies sell less, cut costs, and lay off workers. Unemployment is one of the clearest signs of an economic contraction.
q.cyc_3: What is the 'boom' phase of an economic cycle?
q.cyc_3_a: When the economy reaches its minimum point before recovering
q.cyc_3_b: When GDP falls for two consecutive quarters
q.cyc_3_c: The peak of economic expansion: maximum production, low unemployment, high consumption
q.cyc_3_d: The phase of economic recovery after a crisis
q.cyc_3_exp: The boom is the maximum point of expansion. The economy is hot: jobs, investment, and consumption at their peak. But it can also generate overheating and inflationary pressures.
q.cyc_4: Economic policy can completely eliminate business cycles.
q.cyc_4_exp: Cycles can be mitigated but not eliminated. Recessions are part of the natural dynamics of market economies. Good policy can soften them; bad policy can deepen them.
q.cyc_5: Which of the following is an 'automatic stabilizer' of the economic cycle?
q.cyc_5_exp: Automatic stabilizers work without specific decisions: when the economy falls, unemployment insurance pays out more, cushioning the blow. When it grows, tax revenues increase naturally.
q.fcrisis_1: What was the 2008 global financial crisis triggered by?
q.fcrisis_1_a: A war in the Middle East that cut off oil
q.fcrisis_1_c: The collapse of the U.S. subprime mortgage market and the financial system built on it
q.fcrisis_1_exp: The 2008 crisis started with millions of risky mortgages (subprime) packaged in financial instruments and sold globally. When U.S. real estate fell, the entire system collapsed.
q.fcrisis_2: Financial crises can rapidly spread from one country to others through global financial interconnections.
q.fcrisis_2_exp: Today's financial system is deeply interconnected. A crisis in the U.S. or a large country quickly spreads via banking, trade, and investor confidence. Contagion is a real and rapid phenomenon.
q.fcrisis_3: What was the Great Depression of 1929?
q.fcrisis_3_a: A global recession caused by excessive oil production
q.fcrisis_3_b: A prolonged global economic contraction triggered by the Wall Street stock market crash
q.fcrisis_3_c: A mild slowdown that lasted only 6 months in the U.S.
q.fcrisis_3_d: An industrial crisis limited to Europe after World War I
q.fcrisis_3_exp: The Great Depression was the worst economic crisis of the 20th century. It began with the 1929 stock market crash, causing bank collapses, mass unemployment (25% in the U.S.), and global deflation.
q.fcrisis_4: After a financial crisis, economic recovery typically happens in a few weeks.
q.fcrisis_4_exp: Recoveries after financial crises are usually slow and painful. The 2008 crisis took years to recover from globally. Deep crises destroy confidence, credit, and investment for extended periods.
q.fcrisis_5: What does the concept of 'too big to fail' (TBTF) refer to?
q.fcrisis_5_a: Companies that are too innovative to be regulated
q.fcrisis_5_b: Banks so large that their failure would cause a systemic crisis, forcing the government to rescue them
q.fcrisis_5_c: Multinational companies that are above the law
q.fcrisis_5_d: States that refuse to pay their external debt
q.fcrisis_5_exp: TBTF describes banks whose collapse would be so destabilizing that governments rescue them with public money. It creates a moral hazard: banks take excessive risks knowing they'll be saved.
q.arh_1: What were the main features of Argentina's 2001 economic crisis?
q.arh_1_a: Hyperinflation, fiscal surplus, and low unemployment
q.arh_1_b: Sovereign debt default, banking freeze (corralito), devaluation, and social uprising
q.arh_1_c: Massive capital inflow and GDP growth record
q.arh_1_d: Voluntary debt restructuring with 100% creditor acceptance
q.arh_1_exp: The 2001 crisis involved the corralito (bank freeze), the largest sovereign debt default in history at the time, the devaluation end of convertibility, and the political and social crisis of December 2001.
q.arh_2: Argentina has defaulted on its external debt multiple times throughout its history.
q.arh_2_exp: Argentina has had 9 defaults (or debt restructurings) since its independence. The most notable: 1890, 1982, 2001, 2014 (technical), and 2020. It's one of the most frequent in history.
q.arh_3: What was the main cause of Argentina's 1989 hyperinflation?
q.arh_3_a: An oil shock that raised the cost of all goods
q.arh_3_b: Excessive peso printing to finance fiscal deficit and loss of monetary confidence
q.arh_3_c: A drought that destroyed the entire agricultural harvest
q.arh_3_d: An international embargo that cut off Argentine exports
q.arh_3_exp: Argentina in 1989 combined fiscal deficit, excessive money printing, and complete loss of confidence in the peso. Annual inflation reached 3,079%. Alfonsín had to hand over power to Menem six months early.
q.arh_4: Argentina's economic crises have always been caused by external factors beyond national control.
q.arh_4_exp: Although external factors (rising rates, falling commodity prices) have influenced, Argentina's recurrent crises have deep structural and political roots: fiscal deficits, monetary financing, exchange rate imbalances, and institutional weaknesses.
q.arh_5: What economic lesson did the 2001 crisis leave for Argentina?
q.arh_5_a: That dollar parity is permanently beneficial
q.arh_5_b: That it's better to have no external debt
q.arh_5_c: That rigid exchange rate schemes without fiscal adjustment are unsustainable
q.arh_5_d: That the state should own all banks
q.arh_5_exp: Convertibility collapsed because it required fiscal discipline that Argentina didn't achieve. The lesson: a rigid exchange rate without consistent macroeconomic adjustment generates structural imbalances that end in crisis.
q.prc_1: What financial assets can help protect savings during a crisis?
q.prc_1_a: Keeping all savings in cash under the mattress
q.prc_1_b: Diversified assets: hard currency, government bonds, gold, or indexed instruments
q.prc_1_c: Taking out long-term bank loans at high rates
q.prc_1_d: Investing all savings in a single stock
q.prc_1_exp: In crises, diversification is key: hard currencies (dollar), inflation-indexed bonds (CER), gold, and quality assets in foreign currency offer better protection than concentrating in a single instrument.
q.prc_2: In Argentina, common-sense hedging strategies include holding dollars or CER-adjusted instruments.
q.prc_2_exp: Given recurring devaluations and high inflation, holding dollars is a traditional shelter for Argentines. CER-linked bonds (tied to inflation) protect against purchasing power erosion.
q.prc_3: What is 'capital flight' in the context of economic crises?
q.prc_3_a: The physical destruction of banknotes during a crisis
q.prc_3_b: The process by which the government confiscates private savings
q.prc_3_c: The massive exit of private capital abroad due to distrust in the local economy
q.prc_3_d: The transfer of funds from private banks to the central bank
q.prc_3_exp: Capital flight occurs when individuals and companies move assets abroad seeking security. In Argentina it's a recurring phenomenon: in 2019 alone, $26 billion left the country.
q.prc_4: Maintaining an emergency fund can help cushion the impact of a personal economic crisis.
q.prc_4_exp: An emergency fund (3-6 months of expenses) acts as a financial shock absorber. In times of job loss or economic contraction, it avoids having to go into debt or sell assets at a loss.
q.prc_5: Which of the following is the best general strategy to protect savings from persistent inflation?
q.prc_5_a: Keeping everything in a savings account in pesos at a fixed rate
q.prc_5_b: Spending everything immediately to avoid losing value
q.prc_5_c: Diversifying into hard currency, indexed instruments, and real assets
q.prc_5_d: Keeping everything in cash at home
q.prc_5_exp: Diversification across different inflation-resistant assets (dollar, CER bonds, inflation-adjusted funds, real estate) is the most prudent strategy. Each has its own risk and liquidity tradeoffs.
q.whyb_1: What is the main benefit of having a personal budget?
q.whyb_1_b: Knowing exactly what you earn and spend to make better financial decisions
q.whyb_1_c: Impressing your friends with your financial discipline
q.whyb_1_exp: A budget gives you clarity: where your money comes from and where it goes. Without that information, it's impossible to identify problems or make improvements in your personal finances.
q.whyb_2: A person without a budget can also manage their finances successfully.
q.whyb_2_exp: It's possible, but much harder. Without a budget you don't detect spending leaks, can't plan for goals, and have less control. The budget is the fundamental tool of financial management.
q.whyb_3: What is the difference between a 'need' and a 'want' in budgeting?
q.whyb_3_a: There is no difference; both must be paid
q.whyb_3_b: Needs are essential (food, rent, health); wants are desirable but not essential (streaming, dining out)
q.whyb_3_c: Needs are more expensive than wants
q.whyb_3_exp: Distinguishing needs from wants is the first step in building a budget. Needs are non-negotiable (housing, food); wants can be adjusted if income falls or you want to save more.
q.whyb_4: Budgeting is only useful for people with very low incomes.
q.whyb_4_exp: The budget is useful at all income levels. Even high earners can overspend and end up in debt if they don't manage their finances. The more income, the more important it is to plan where it goes.
q.whyb_5: Which of the following is the first step to building a useful personal budget?
q.whyb_5_b: Calculating and recording your income and all expenses for at least one month
q.whyb_5_d: Asking a financial advisor to manage your money
q.whyb_5_exp: Before making decisions, you need accurate data. Recording income and all expenses for a month gives you a real baseline to analyze, identify leaks, and plan realistic adjustments.
q.bb_1: What does the 50/30/20 budget rule establish?
q.bb_1_b: 50% needs, 30% wants, 20% savings and debt repayment
q.bb_1_d: 50% for housing, 30% for food, 20% for everything else
q.bb_1_exp: The 50/30/20 rule (popularized by Elizabeth Warren) suggests allocating 50% of net income to needs, 30% to wants, and 20% to savings and/or debt repayment. It's a flexible starting framework.
q.bb_2: The 50/30/20 rule is applicable regardless of the level of inflation in the economy.
q.bb_2_exp: With very high inflation (like in Argentina), proportions need adjustment. More than 50% may go to basic needs, making 20% savings harder to achieve. The rule is a guide, not a rigid rule.
q.bb_3: What are 'fixed expenses' in a personal budget?
q.bb_3_a: Those that are paid in cash only
q.bb_3_b: Those that are always paid in the same month of the year
q.bb_3_c: Those that recur each month with the same or very similar amount (rent, installments, subscriptions)
q.bb_3_exp: Fixed expenses are predictable and recurring: rent, loan installments, fixed subscriptions. Knowing them lets you plan accurately because they won't change much month to month.
q.bb_4: Variable expenses are more difficult to control than fixed expenses.
q.bb_4_exp: Variable expenses (food, entertainment, clothing) change each month and are harder to track. But they are also more controllable: it's easier to cut discretionary spending than to renegotiate rent.
q.bb_5: What does it mean to have a 'balanced' personal budget?
q.bb_5_a: Having all your money in the same bank
q.bb_5_b: Spending exactly the same as the month before
q.bb_5_c: That income covers all expenses and there's money left over for savings
q.bb_5_d: That income and expenses are equal, with zero leftover
q.bb_5_exp: A balanced budget means income ≥ expenses, ideally with a positive surplus to save or invest. If income = expenses with zero surplus, there's no room for emergencies or future goals.
q.ante_1_a: Large occasional expenses like vacations or appliances
q.ante_1_b: Small daily expenses that individually seem insignificant but accumulate a lot over the month
q.ante_1_c: Fixed monthly expenses like rent or subscriptions
q.ante_1_d: Work-related expenses that you can deduct from taxes
q.ante_1_exp: Ant expenses are small daily purchases (coffees, snacks, small impulse buys). Individually harmless, collectively they can represent thousands of pesos per month. Awareness is the first step.
q.ante_2: Controlling ant expenses can free up significant money at the end of the month.
q.ante_2_exp: A $500 daily coffee = $15,000 per month. Multiply by several small daily expenses and you quickly have significant amounts. Awareness and slight adjustment can free up real money.
q.ante_3: What is a common method to identify your own 'money leaks'?
q.ante_3_a: Asking your bank to freeze your accounts
q.ante_3_b: Tracking all expenses for 30 days, including the smallest ones
q.ante_3_d: Only using cash for all purchases
q.ante_3_exp: Recording all expenses for a month (including coffees, tips, small digital purchases) reveals patterns you didn't notice. Awareness of leaks is the first step to plugging them.
q.ante_4: Subscription costs (streaming, apps, gyms) are usually irrelevant to the personal budget.
q.ante_4_exp: Accumulated subscriptions can be a significant monthly expense. An unused gym + Netflix + Spotify + Disney + delivery apps can add up to thousands. Reviewing them regularly is good financial hygiene.
q.ante_5: Which of the following is the best strategy to control daily impulse spending?
q.ante_5_b: Using a spending journal or app to record each purchase
q.ante_5_exp: Tracking spending in real time (notebook, app, spreadsheet) creates awareness. The simple act of recording a purchase makes you more thoughtful before doing it. It's one of the most effective habits in personal finance.
q.sinstr_1: What is the main purpose of saving?
q.sinstr_1_a: Impressing others with your financial discipline
q.sinstr_1_b: Accumulating resources for future goals or financial emergencies
q.sinstr_1_exp: Saving allows you to build a financial cushion: for emergencies, medium-term goals (travel, renovation), and long-term goals (retirement, children's education). Without saving, you're vulnerable to any setback.
q.sinstr_2: The best savings strategy is always to save what's 'left over' at the end of the month.
q.sinstr_2_exp: Saving 'what's left over' usually ends up being zero. The recommended strategy is 'pay yourself first': set aside your savings percentage at the beginning of the month, before spending.
q.sinstr_3: What is a 'fixed-term deposit' as a savings instrument?
q.sinstr_3_a: A bank loan at a fixed rate
q.sinstr_3_b: An investment fund that invests in stocks
q.sinstr_3_c: A bank account where you deposit money for a specific period and receive interest at maturity
q.sinstr_3_exp: The traditional bank term deposit: you deposit for 30, 60, or 90 days and receive interest at the end. In Argentina it's common but usually below inflation, so it doesn't fully protect the real value.
q.sinstr_4: Money market mutual funds (FCI money market) in Argentina typically offer returns close to or above the policy rate.
q.sinstr_4_exp: FCIs T+0 (immediate redemption) invest in short-term instruments. They offer competitive rates with maximum liquidity: you can withdraw any day. They're one of the most popular alternatives to traditional fixed-term deposits.
q.sinstr_5: Which of the following saving criteria is most important to evaluate before choosing an instrument?
q.sinstr_5_a: Brand name of the bank or institution
q.sinstr_5_b: Liquidity, return, and risk according to your time horizon
q.sinstr_5_c: The amount of advertising the product has
q.sinstr_5_d: That the product is new on the market
q.sinstr_5_exp: Any savings instrument should be evaluated on three axes: liquidity (can you withdraw when needed?), return (does it beat inflation?), and risk (how much can you lose?). The right combination depends on your personal goal.
q.swi_1: What is the main purpose of an emergency fund?
q.swi_1_b: Having liquid resources to face unexpected events without going into debt
q.swi_1_c: Investing in shares when the market falls
q.swi_1_d: Covering future car or appliance purchases
q.swi_1_exp: The emergency fund is your financial safety net: job loss, medical expenses, urgent repairs. Its goal is NOT to invest or generate returns, but to be immediately available in a real emergency.
q.swi_2: Financial experts recommend that an emergency fund cover between 3 and 6 months of essential expenses.
q.swi_2_exp: 3-6 months gives you enough time to find a new job, recover from an illness, or face an unexpected expense without falling into debt. Less = too exposed; more = excessive opportunity cost.
q.swi_3: What is the ideal instrument to keep an emergency fund?
q.swi_3_a: In long-term investments like stocks or bonds
q.swi_3_b: In immediately accessible instruments (savings account, money market FCI)
q.swi_3_exp: The emergency fund must be: 1) liquid (accessible in 24-48 hours), 2) low risk (no volatility), and 3) in a stable currency or inflation-adjusted instrument. It's not for investing; it's for protecting.
q.swi_4: Using the emergency fund for a planned purchase (like a trip) defeats its purpose.
q.swi_4_exp: The emergency fund is sacred: only for real unexpected events. Spending it on planned purchases leaves you unprotected. For planned goals (travel, renovation) you should have a separate specific fund.
q.swi_5: How should you prioritize building an emergency fund relative to other financial goals?
q.swi_5_a: After investing in stocks and crypto
q.swi_5_b: Only when you have significant income
q.swi_5_c: As a first priority, before any investment or non-essential spending
q.swi_5_d: It's not necessary if you have a credit card
q.swi_5_exp: The emergency fund is the base of any solid financial plan. Without it, any setback (layoff, medical bill) can derail all your other financial goals. Build it first, even if you start small.
q.gbd_1: What is the difference between 'good debt' and 'bad debt'?
q.gbd_1_a: Good debt is small; bad debt is large
q.gbd_1_b: Good debt is from banks; bad debt is from private lenders
q.gbd_1_c: Good debt finances assets or generates future value; bad debt finances consumption that depreciates
q.gbd_1_d: There's no useful distinction; all debt is bad
q.gbd_1_exp: Good debt: a mortgage to buy a home, a loan for education or to start a business that can generate income. Bad debt: financing a vacation or electronics on a credit card at 200% annual interest.
q.gbd_2: A mortgage to buy a primary home is generally considered 'good debt'.
q.gbd_2_exp: If the rate is reasonable and the property doesn't lose value, a mortgage builds equity over time. You're paying for an asset you'll own, instead of paying rent to a third party forever.
q.gbd_3: What does a loan's 'CFT' (Total Financial Cost) measure?
q.gbd_3_a: The bank's commission for opening the account
q.gbd_3_b: The total real cost of a loan, including interest, fees, and administrative expenses
q.gbd_3_d: The credit limit granted by the bank
q.gbd_3_exp: The CFT is the true indicator of how much a loan costs. It's always higher than the nominal rate because it includes fees, insurance, and other charges. In Argentina it's the mandatory figure to compare loans.
q.gbd_4: Taking out a loan in pesos at a fixed rate during a period of high inflation can be beneficial for the borrower.
q.gbd_4_exp: Counterintuitive but true: if inflation is 100% and your fixed-rate loan is at 60%, you're paying back with money worth half as much. The real value of the debt falls. That's why fixed-rate debt can be advantageous in inflationary periods.
q.gbd_5: Which of the following is the best way to evaluate whether to take on debt?
q.gbd_5_a: If the monthly payment fits in the budget, it's automatically good debt
q.gbd_5_b: Comparing the loan's CFT with what the loan will generate (return, savings, or appreciation)
q.gbd_5_c: Taking the loan from the bank that offers the most advertising
q.gbd_5_d: Always avoiding debt regardless of the circumstances
q.gbd_5_exp: Good financial decision: compare the cost of debt (CFT) vs. what you gain (investment return, appreciation, income generated). If return > cost, the debt can make sense. If not, it's better to avoid it.
q.ccard_1: How does a credit card work?
q.ccard_1_a: You borrow money from the bank and pay it back with interest every month
q.ccard_1_b: You spend money you already have in your bank account
q.ccard_1_c: The card company pays your purchases and you repay them, ideally in full at month end
q.ccard_1_d: It's equivalent to having a savings account at the bank
q.ccard_1_exp: A credit card is short-term credit: you buy now and pay the full balance at month end (zero interest) or pay the minimum (generating very high interest). The trap is only paying the minimum month after month.
q.ccard_2: Paying only the 'minimum payment' on a credit card is one of the most expensive forms of consumer debt.
q.ccard_2_exp: Minimum payments barely cover interest. With annual rates of 150-300% in Argentina, a $10,000 balance can more than double in a year if you only pay minimums. It's a debt trap.
q.ccard_3: What is the 'billing cycle' on a credit card?
q.ccard_3_a: The maximum amount you can spend on the card
q.ccard_3_b: The interest-free period between purchase and payment due date
q.ccard_3_c: The monthly period in which purchases are accumulated and a summary is generated
q.ccard_3_d: The number of installments to pay a purchase
q.ccard_3_exp: The billing cycle is typically monthly. Purchases made during the cycle appear in the summary and must be paid by the due date. Using the closing date strategically gives you more interest-free time.
q.ccard_4: In Argentina, buying in 'fixed installments' (cuotas sin interés) can be an advantage in high-inflation contexts.
q.ccard_4_exp: If the installment doesn't have interest and inflation is high, you're paying back with pesos worth less each month. The merchant absorbs this cost. That's why no-interest installments are popular in Argentina.
q.ccard_5: What is the main risk of using credit cards without financial control?
q.ccard_5_a: Losing your ability to buy in installments
q.ccard_5_b: Accumulating debt at very high interest rates that grow exponentially
q.ccard_5_c: Not being able to use online banking
q.ccard_5_exp: The main risk: spending beyond your means and not being able to pay the full balance. With rates above 150% annually, unpaid credit card debt grows exponentially and can become unmanageable quickly.
q.pln_1: What is the 'CFT' that banks must show on personal loan advertising?
