q.pln_1_b: The total financial cost: the true total rate including interest, fees, and insurance
q.pln_1_exp: The CFT (Total Financial Cost) is the real indicator of loan cost. It's always higher than the nominal rate. In Argentina, the BCRA requires banks to publish it. Always compare CFTs, not just nominal rates.
q.pln_2: Personal loans in Argentina typically have higher interest rates than mortgage loans.
q.pln_2_exp: Personal loans are riskier for banks (no collateral) and shorter term, so their rates are higher. Mortgages are secured by the property, which reduces the bank's risk and lowers the rate.
q.pln_3: What is the 'debt-to-income ratio' and why is it important?
q.pln_3_a: The ratio between your total debt and your annual income, used to evaluate repayment capacity
q.pln_3_b: The interest rate compared to your salary
q.pln_3_c: The ratio between fixed and variable expenses
q.pln_3_d: The number of debts you have divided by your monthly income
q.pln_3_exp: Banks typically recommend not committing more than 30-35% of income to debt repayments. Above that level, you face difficulty covering other essential expenses and maintaining your quality of life.
q.pln_4: In Argentina, taking out a personal loan in pesos at a very high nominal rate can make sense if it allows accessing a good that will quickly appreciate or generate income.
q.pln_4_exp: Context matters: if buying equipment for a business that will generate more than the loan cost, taking it on makes sense. The key is comparing the return vs. the CFT. Never borrow just because the installment 'fits'.
q.pln_5: Which of the following questions should you ask before taking any personal loan?
q.pln_5_a: Which bank has the most ATMs
q.pln_5_b: How much is the maximum credit limit
q.pln_5_c: What is the CFT and how does the total cost compare to what you'll obtain
q.pln_5_d: Whether the bank is well-known on social media
q.pln_5_exp: Before any loan: What does it cost me in total? (CFT × term), Why am I taking it? (necessity vs. whim), Will what I get generate more value than the cost? Good financial decisions require this analysis.
q.god_1: What does the 'debt snowball' method propose for getting out of debt?
q.god_1_a: Paying off the highest-interest debt first
q.god_1_c: Paying off the smallest debt first to gain psychological momentum
q.god_1_d: Ignoring all debts and waiting for them to expire
q.god_1_exp: The snowball method (Dave Ramsey) pays off the smallest debt first. It generates quick victories and motivation. The 'avalanche' method pays the highest rate first, which is mathematically cheaper.
q.god_2: Debt consolidation can reduce total monthly payments by combining multiple debts into one lower-rate loan.
q.god_2_exp: Consolidation groups several debts (credit cards, small loans) into one. If you get a lower rate, you pay less interest. But be careful: if the term extends, you can end up paying more in total.
q.god_3: What is the main risk of 'debt consolidation'?
q.god_3_b: Having to pay all debts at once
q.god_3_c: Extending the term and paying more interest in total, or quickly accumulating new debt
q.god_3_exp: Consolidating frees up monthly cash flow, but if the new term is much longer, you pay more in total. And if you don't address the behavior that generated the debt, you'll quickly reaccumulate new debt.
q.god_4: Negotiating with creditors to get lower interest rates or payment plans is a valid strategy to manage debt.
q.god_4_exp: Many creditors would rather negotiate than have you default. In Argentina, banks often offer refinancing or interest reduction programs. It's always worth calling and asking before falling behind on payments.
q.god_5: Which of the following is the most important first step to get out of debt?
q.god_5_a: Taking out a new loan to pay off existing debts
q.god_5_c: Listing all debts, rates, and balances, and creating a plan to attack them
q.god_5_d: Waiting for a salary increase before starting
q.god_5_exp: You can't solve what you don't know. The first step is a complete inventory: what do I owe? to whom? at what rate? what's the total balance? Only with that information can you build a real and effective plan.
q.wii_1: What is the fundamental difference between saving and investing?
q.wii_1_a: Saving is only for the poor; investing is for the rich
q.wii_1_b: Saving preserves capital with minimal risk; investing seeks growth, accepting higher risk
q.wii_1_c: Saving is always in pesos; investing is always in dollars
q.wii_1_d: There's no real difference between the two
q.wii_1_exp: Saving = preserving what you have, with little risk and limited return. Investing = putting capital to work to generate return, accepting the possibility of losing some or all of it. Both are necessary in a financial plan.
q.wii_2: Any investment involves some level of risk, even if it appears 'safe'.
q.wii_2_exp: Even a term deposit in a bank carries risk (bank failure, inflation that erodes real value). The concept of 'risk-free' doesn't really exist. What changes is the type and magnitude of risk.
q.wii_3: What does 'return' mean in the context of investments?
q.wii_3_a: The amount paid to the bank as commission
q.wii_3_b: The profit or gain generated by an investment relative to the initial amount invested
q.wii_3_c: The interest rate charged for a personal loan
q.wii_3_d: The total value of an investment portfolio
q.wii_3_exp: Return measures how much an investment generated. If you invest $100 and get back $115, the return is 15%. Comparing returns with inflation and other options lets you evaluate if the investment was really profitable.
q.wii_4: In general, investments with higher potential returns carry higher risk.
q.wii_4_exp: This is one of the fundamental principles of finance: risk-return tradeoff. If someone offers you a 'safe' investment with exceptional returns, be suspicious. Higher returns always come with higher risk.
q.wii_5: What is 'real return' as opposed to nominal return?
q.wii_5_a: Return in dollars vs. return in pesos
q.wii_5_c: Nominal return minus inflation: what the investment actually gained in purchasing power
q.wii_5_exp: If your investment returned 80% but inflation was 100%, your real return is negative: you lost purchasing power. In Argentina, always think in real returns (vs. inflation), not just nominal.
q.rr_1_a: Putting all your money in the best performing asset
q.rr_1_b: Distributing capital across different assets and markets to reduce concentration risk
q.rr_1_c: Buying and selling rapidly to capture short-term gains
q.rr_1_exp: Diversification reduces idiosyncratic risk: if one asset falls, others may compensate. A diversified portfolio across stocks, bonds, currencies, and sectors is less vulnerable than one concentrated in a single asset.
q.rr_2: An investor with a high tolerance for losses (risk tolerance) can allocate a larger portion to more volatile assets like stocks.
q.rr_2_exp: Risk tolerance depends on age, financial situation, goals, and temperament. Young people with long investment horizons can afford more volatility because they have time to recover. Near retirement, the opposite.
q.rr_3: What is 'volatility' in the context of investments?
q.rr_3_a: The frequency with which you buy and sell assets
q.rr_3_b: The variation in returns: how much an investment's value moves up and down
q.rr_3_c: The commission charged by the broker
q.rr_3_d: The difference between local and international interest rates
q.rr_3_exp: Volatility measures how much an asset's price fluctuates. High volatility = large and rapid movements (cryptocurrencies, emerging market stocks). Low volatility = more stable (short-term bonds, money market funds).
q.rr_4: Investing in a single company that is performing excellently is the best strategy to maximize returns.
q.rr_4_exp: Concentration in one company maximizes return IF it performs well, but also maximizes loss if it doesn't. That's why professionals recommend diversification: you sacrifice the upside of the 'winner' to avoid the downside of the 'loser'.
q.rr_5_a: The geographic limit of where you can invest
q.rr_5_b: The maximum amount you can invest
q.rr_5_c: The estimated time you plan to maintain an investment before needing the money
q.rr_5_d: The interest rate on long-term bonds
q.rr_5_exp: The investment horizon is key: a 20-year horizon allows more risk (time to recover from falls). A 1-year horizon requires more conservative instruments. Horizon determines the appropriate portfolio.
q.fci_1: What is a mutual fund (FCI - Fondo Común de Inversión) in Argentina?
q.fci_1_a: A government savings account that pays interest
q.fci_1_b: A pool of money from many investors, managed professionally and invested in financial assets
q.fci_1_c: A bank loan for productive investment
q.fci_1_d: A type of cryptocurrency issued by the central bank
q.fci_1_exp: An FCI pools money from many investors. A professional manager invests it in stocks, bonds, currencies, etc. Each investor owns 'cuotapartes' proportional to their contribution. It allows diversifying with small capital.
q.fci_2: Money market FCIs (T+0) allow investors to withdraw their money the same day.
q.fci_2_exp: FCIs T+0 have immediate redemption (same day or 24 hours). They invest in short-term instruments (money market). They're very popular in Argentina as an alternative to fixed deposits with more flexibility.
q.fci_3: What does 'cuotaparte' mean in a mutual fund?
q.fci_3_a: The monthly fee charged by the fund manager
q.fci_3_b: The fraction of the fund's total assets that corresponds to each investor
q.fci_3_c: The minimum amount required to invest
q.fci_3_exp: A cuotaparte is like a share of the fund. Its value fluctuates with the assets the fund holds. If you buy 100 cuotapartes at $100 each and they rise to $110, your investment is now worth $11,000.
q.fci_4: All mutual funds in Argentina guarantee a minimum return.
q.fci_4_exp: FCIs do NOT guarantee returns (except some special structures). Their returns depend on the performance of the assets they invest in. Money market FCIs are more stable; equity FCIs fluctuate much more.
q.fci_5: What is the main advantage of investing in FCIs for a small investor?
q.fci_5_a: Being able to access investments that normally require large amounts of capital
q.fci_5_d: Having priority over other investors when withdrawing funds
q.fci_5_exp: FCIs democratize access: with small capital you can be part of a diversified portfolio managed professionally. Without FCIs, diversifying a small portfolio would be very expensive and complex.
q.bon_1: What is a bond in financial terms?
q.bon_1_a: A share of a company traded on the stock exchange
q.bon_1_b: A certificate of ownership of a business
q.bon_1_c: A debt instrument: you lend money to an issuer who commits to repay it with interest
q.bon_1_d: A type of structured deposit in a bank
q.bon_1_exp: A bond is a loan from the investor to the issuer (government or company). The issuer promises to pay regular interest (coupon) and return the principal at maturity. It's fixed income.
q.bon_2: Argentina has defaulted on its sovereign debt multiple times, making its bonds high-risk investments.
q.bon_2_exp: Argentina has 9 defaults. Its bonds trade with high yields precisely because the market demands extra compensation for the high risk. High yield = high risk. They're not for conservative investors.
q.bon_3: What is the 'coupon' of a bond?
q.bon_3_a: The commission charged by the broker for the purchase
q.bon_3_b: The discount applied to the purchase price
q.bon_3_c: The periodic interest payment made by the issuer to the bondholder
q.bon_3_d: The difference between the issuance price and the market price
q.bon_3_exp: The coupon is the interest the bond pays at regular intervals (semi-annually, quarterly). If a bond has a 6% annual coupon and a face value of $1,000, it pays $60 per year.
q.bon_4: Bond prices and their yields move in opposite directions.
q.bon_4_exp: When a bond's price rises, its effective yield (yield to maturity) falls, and vice versa. It's a mathematical inverse relationship: if you pay more for the same fixed cash flows, your return percentage is lower.
q.bon_5: What does it mean that a bond is 'CER adjusted' in Argentina?
q.bon_5_a: That it pays interest in dollars
q.bon_5_b: That its capital and interest are adjusted by the CER coefficient (inflation index)
q.bon_5_c: That it has a guaranteed return regardless of inflation
q.bon_5_d: That it can only be purchased through the BCRA
q.bon_5_exp: CER bonds are indexed to inflation (CER = Reference Stabilization Coefficient, based on CPI). They protect purchasing power: if inflation is 100%, the bond principal and coupons also rise ~100%.
q.etfp_1_a: A type of bank savings account
q.etfp_1_b: A fund that trades on the stock exchange like a stock and tracks an index or asset basket
q.etfp_1_exp: An ETF is a fund traded on the exchange that replicates the performance of an index (S&P 500, NASDAQ, gold, etc.). It combines the diversification of a fund with the flexibility of buying/selling like a stock.
q.etfp_2: ETFs generally have lower management fees than actively managed mutual funds.
q.etfp_2_exp: Most ETFs are passive (they replicate an index without active management), so their fees are much lower. S&P 500 ETFs like VOO or SPY charge less than 0.1% annual management fee.
q.etfp_3: What does a 'diversified portfolio' typically include?
q.etfp_3_b: A mix of assets: stocks, bonds, currencies, commodities, and geographic diversification
q.etfp_3_d: Only government bonds from the home country
q.etfp_3_exp: A well-diversified portfolio spreads risk across different asset types (stocks, bonds, real assets), geographies (domestic and international), and currencies. Each component has different correlations, reducing total volatility.
q.etfp_4: In Argentina, CEDEARs allow investing in foreign company stocks and ETFs without having to open an account abroad.
q.etfp_4_exp: CEDEARs are certificates that represent foreign shares (Apple, Google, S&P 500 ETF) traded on the ByMA in pesos. They're a popular way to dollarize a portfolio without leaving the local system.
q.etfp_5: What is the main advantage of a portfolio strategy over investing in individual stocks?
q.etfp_5_d: Better risk management through diversification, reducing dependence on any single asset
q.etfp_5_exp: A portfolio strategy reduces specific risk: if one company fails, the rest of the portfolio cushions the impact. You sacrifice some potential gain, but you trade it for much more stability and predictability.
q.stab_1_a: A very stable traditional cryptocurrency with low volatility
q.stab_1_b: A cryptocurrency whose value is pegged to a stable asset, usually the US dollar
q.stab_1_c: A digital bond issued by the central bank
q.stab_1_d: A bank savings account in foreign currency
q.stab_1_exp: Stablecoins maintain their value tied to an external asset (usually the dollar). Examples: USDT (Tether), USDC (Circle), DAI. They offer the accessibility of crypto without extreme volatility.
q.stab_2: USDT (Tether) is a stablecoin backed by dollar reserves.
q.stab_2_exp: Tether claims to maintain 1 USDT = 1 USD through dollar reserves and equivalents. Its transparency has been questioned, but it's the most widely used stablecoin globally with hundreds of billions in capitalization.
q.stab_3: What is the main use of stablecoins in Argentina?
q.stab_3_b: Digitally dollarizing savings and making international transfers without traditional banking
q.stab_3_c: Paying taxes to AFIP in foreign currency
q.stab_3_exp: In Argentina, stablecoins allow 'digital dollarization': having dollars accessible in crypto wallets, outside the traditional banking system and exchange controls. Very useful for protecting purchasing power.
q.stab_4: All stablecoins are equally safe and their dollar peg is guaranteed.
q.stab_4_exp: Not all stablecoins are equal. Algorithmic stablecoins (like TerraUST) can collapse catastrophically. Even centralized stablecoins have counterparty risks. Not all are backed 1:1 with real dollars.
q.stab_5: What distinguishes a 'collateralized' stablecoin from an 'algorithmic' one?
q.stab_5_a: Collateralized stablecoins are decentralized; algorithmic are centralized
q.stab_5_b: Collateralized stablecoins are backed by real assets; algorithmic ones maintain parity through code mechanisms
q.stab_5_c: Algorithmic stablecoins are always safer because they don't depend on a company
q.stab_5_d: There's no practical difference between the two
q.stab_5_exp: Collateralized (USDC, USDT): backed by real dollars or assets. Algorithmic (TerraUST, failed in 2022): maintained parity through code and incentives. The algorithmic model proved much more fragile.
q.defi_1_a: A traditional bank that operates online
q.defi_1_b: A set of financial services that operate on blockchain without central intermediaries
q.defi_1_d: A crypto exchange regulated by the central bank
q.defi_1_exp: DeFi uses smart contracts on blockchain (mainly Ethereum) to offer lending, exchange, and savings services without banks. It's open, permissionless, and accessible from anywhere in the world.
q.defi_2: Smart contracts execute automatically when their conditions are met, without needing human intermediaries.
q.defi_2_exp: Smart contracts are programs on the blockchain that automatically execute when their conditions are met. Example: 'release funds when both parties sign.' They eliminate middlemen and reduce trust costs.
q.defi_3_a: A traditional crypto exchange regulated by governments
q.defi_3_b: A decentralized exchange where users trade directly from their wallets without an intermediary
q.defi_3_c: A tool for mining new cryptocurrencies
q.defi_3_exp: DEXs (Uniswap, dYdX, Curve) allow exchanging crypto without a centralized intermediary. Users connect their wallet and trade directly with automated liquidity pools. Greater privacy but more risk.
q.defi_4: DeFi protocols are exempt from hacks and smart contract vulnerabilities.
q.defi_4_exp: DeFi has suffered billions in losses from hacks, bugs in smart contracts, and exploits. The absence of an intermediary also means there's no one to call if something goes wrong. High potential = high risk.
q.defi_5: What is 'yield farming' in the DeFi ecosystem?
q.defi_5_b: Providing liquidity to DeFi protocols in exchange for rewards in the form of tokens
q.defi_5_c: Buying and selling crypto very frequently
q.defi_5_exp: Yield farming involves providing liquidity to DeFi protocols (lending, pools) and receiving rewards in the form of tokens. Attractive returns but with risks of smart contracts, volatility, and 'impermanent loss'.
q.clatam_1: What is one of the main reasons for high crypto adoption in Latin America?
q.clatam_1_a: Excessive government regulation that forces its use
q.clatam_1_b: High financial stability of local currencies
q.clatam_1_c: Currency devaluation, inflation, and banking restrictions that drive people to seek alternatives
q.clatam_1_d: Free distribution of cryptocurrencies by local governments
q.clatam_1_exp: Countries like Argentina, Venezuela, and Colombia lead global crypto adoption per capita. Main reason: distrust in the local currency, inflation, restrictions to buying dollars, and interest in financial alternatives.
q.clatam_2: El Salvador was the first country in the world to adopt Bitcoin as legal tender.
q.clatam_2_exp: In 2021, El Salvador under Nayib Bukele became the first country to recognize Bitcoin as legal tender alongside the dollar. The experiment has been controversial, with mixed economic results.
q.clatam_3: What makes Argentina one of the countries with the highest crypto use in the region?
q.clatam_3_a: Strong cryptocurrency regulation that protects investors
q.clatam_3_b: The combination of chronic inflation, exchange controls, and distrust of the local banking system
q.clatam_3_c: Massive government subsidies for crypto adoption
q.clatam_3_d: Very low internet prices that facilitate access
q.clatam_3_exp: Argentina is in the global top 10 for crypto adoption. The combination of triple-digit inflation, recurrent devaluations, exchange controls (cepo), and distrust in banks drives many Argentines to seek refuge in crypto and stablecoins.
q.clatam_4: Cryptocurrencies in Latin America are mainly used for pure speculation rather than as a store of value or remittances.
q.clatam_4_exp: In the region, crypto has practical uses: storing value in dollars (stablecoins), sending international remittances faster and cheaper, and accessing DeFi services for the unbanked. Speculation is part of it, but not the only use.
q.clatam_5: Which of the following is the most common practical use of stablecoins in Argentina?
q.clatam_5_a: Paying salaries in the public sector
q.clatam_5_b: Protecting savings against peso devaluation, equivalent to having digital dollars
q.clatam_5_c: Paying for public services like electricity or water
q.clatam_5_exp: USDT and USDC are used by many Argentines as a practical form of 'digital dollar': they protect the value of savings against peso depreciation, accessible 24/7 and outside traditional banking restrictions.
q.taxinv_1: In Argentina, are gains from stock investments taxed?
q.taxinv_1_a: No, stock investments are completely exempt
q.taxinv_1_b: Yes, capital gains tax applies, with different rates by type of asset and residence
q.taxinv_1_c: Only if the investor earned more than $1 million pesos
q.taxinv_1_d: Only foreign investors pay taxes on local market gains
q.taxinv_1_exp: In Argentina, individual investors pay capital gains tax on financial investments. The rate varies: Argentine residents pay 5% on CER bonds and dollar-linked assets; 15% on foreign assets or dollar-denominated bonds.
q.taxinv_2: In Argentina, CER-adjusted bonds generate taxable capital gains for resident investors.
q.taxinv_2_exp: CER-adjusted bonds and others issued in pesos pay a reduced capital gains rate (5%) for Argentine residents. Each type of asset has its own rate; consulting a tax advisor before building a portfolio is recommended.
q.taxinv_3: What is the 'bienes personales' (personal assets tax) in Argentina?
q.taxinv_3_a: A tax on the purchase of imported goods
q.taxinv_3_b: An annual tax on an individual's total net assets above a minimum threshold
q.taxinv_3_c: A tax on salaries paid by employers
q.taxinv_3_d: A VAT surcharge on personal services
q.taxinv_3_exp: Bienes Personales taxes net assets (financial investments, real estate, vehicles, etc.) above a minimum non-taxable amount. Financial assets include stocks, bonds, and FCIs. Rates vary by total patrimony.
q.taxinv_4: Cryptocurrency gains are exempt from income tax in Argentina.
q.taxinv_4_exp: As of recent regulations, cryptocurrency gains are subject to capital gains tax in Argentina. Argentine residents: 15% on crypto and foreign digital assets. The legal framework is still evolving; consult updated sources.
q.taxinv_5: What is the advantage of investing through tax-friendly instruments like PGC (individual savings plans in other countries) in Argentina?
q.taxinv_5_a: Generating guaranteed higher returns than traditional investments
q.taxinv_5_b: Having fiscal benefits that reduce taxes on returns, allowing more money to compound
q.taxinv_5_d: Enabling investment with money that was not declared
q.taxinv_5_exp: Tax-advantaged instruments (where available) reduce the tax burden on returns. Less tax = more capital that compounds over time. In Argentina there's no exact equivalent yet, but there are tax-advantaged options for retirement savings.
q.ret_1: What is the main challenge facing Argentina's pension system (SIPA)?
q.ret_1_b: The inverted demographic pyramid and an increasing ratio of retirees to active contributors
q.ret_1_c: Very high retirement pensions compared to developed countries
q.ret_1_d: Excessive government investment in the system
q.ret_1_exp: Argentina has one retiree for every 1.5-2 active contributors (the ratio should ideally be 4:1). An aging population + high informality = growing fiscal pressure on the pension system.
q.ret_2: Relying solely on the state pension system is a sufficient strategy for a financially stable retirement in Argentina.
q.ret_2_exp: Argentine pensions typically replace only 40-60% of final salary, often below poverty lines. The recommendation: build complementary savings (voluntary pension plans, long-term investments) independently of the state system.
q.ret_3: What is 'compound interest' and why is it key for long-term retirement savings?
q.ret_3_a: A fee that banks charge for managing pension funds
q.ret_3_b: A penalty for early withdrawal from a savings plan
q.ret_3_c: Interest that generates returns on itself over time, making capital grow exponentially with patience
q.ret_3_d: A bond index that adjusts for inflation
q.ret_3_exp: Compound interest is Einstein's 'eighth wonder': returns generate more returns. $1,000 at 10% annual becomes $1,100 in year 1; in year 2, you earn 10% on $1,100, not just $1,000. Over 30+ years, the effect is transformative.
q.ret_4: Starting to save for retirement at 25 vs. at 35 makes little practical difference.
q.ret_4_exp: Time is the most valuable variable in compound interest. Starting at 25 vs. 35 can mean having double or triple the capital at retirement. 10 extra years of compounding generates enormous differences.
q.ret_5: What percentage of income do financial experts typically recommend saving long-term for retirement?
q.ret_5_c: It depends entirely on the person's age when they start, but commonly 10-15%
q.ret_5_exp: The commonly cited benchmark is 10-15% of income toward retirement. The exact percentage depends on when you start and what lifestyle you want in retirement. Starting early allows saving less each month due to the power of compounding.
q.insur_1: What is the fundamental purpose of insurance?
q.insur_1_b: Transferring a financial risk to a third party (the insurer) in exchange for a periodic premium
q.insur_1_c: Guaranteeing that covered events will never happen
q.insur_1_exp: Insurance is risk transfer: you pay a small, certain amount (premium) to avoid a large, uncertain loss. You don't buy it hoping to use it; you buy it so a catastrophic event doesn't destroy your financial plan.
q.insur_2: Not having health insurance is a valid financial option for young, healthy people.
q.insur_2_exp: While the probability of serious illness is low at a young age, a medical emergency without coverage can be financially catastrophic. Young people get in accidents, have appendicitis, or break bones. One hospitalization can wipe out years of savings.
q.insur_3: What does a life insurance policy typically cover?
q.insur_3_a: Medical expenses from illness or accidents
q.insur_3_b: Losses from investments in the stock market
q.insur_3_c: Payment of a sum to designated beneficiaries upon the insured's death
q.insur_3_d: Protection against theft of personal property
q.insur_3_exp: Life insurance pays a sum to beneficiaries (spouse, children, parents) when the insured dies. It's especially important if you have financial dependents: people who would be affected by your absence.
q.insur_4: In Argentina, mandatory car insurance (Seguro contra terceros) only covers damages caused to third parties, not to your own vehicle.
q.insur_4_exp: Third-party liability is mandatory by law and only covers damages you cause to others. Comprehensive insurance (Seguro contra todo riesgo) also covers your own vehicle. For financed or newer cars, comprehensive is recommended.
q.insur_5: Which insurance should generally be prioritized first in a personal financial plan?
q.insur_5_c: Health insurance and life insurance (if you have dependents)
q.insur_5_exp: Health and life insurance protect against catastrophic and unrecoverable risks. They're the financial priority before worrying about smaller risks. The principle: insure against what you can't afford to lose.
q.homef_1: What is the most important principle for maintaining healthy household finances?
q.homef_1_a: Spending 100% of income every month to stimulate the economy
q.homef_1_b: That household income is permanently greater than expenses, with room for savings
q.homef_1_c: Having as many credit cards as possible
q.homef_1_d: Delegating all financial decisions to the bank
q.homef_1_exp: The golden rule: income > expenses, with a positive margin. That surplus allows saving, covering emergencies, and working toward goals. A household that spends everything it earns is one paycheck away from financial crisis.
q.homef_2: Joint household finances tend to work better when both members have visibility and participation in financial decisions.
q.homef_2_exp: Financial opacity in couples generates conflict and bad decisions. Studies show that households where both members participate in financial planning save more, take on less unproductive debt, and are more aligned in their financial goals.
q.homef_3: What is the best way to manage a household budget with multiple members?
q.homef_3_a: The member who earns most makes all financial decisions unilaterally
q.homef_3_b: Each member manages their money independently with no shared coordination
q.homef_3_c: Define clear rules for shared expenses, each person's contributions, and savings goals
q.homef_3_d: Always use a single bank account for everything
q.homef_3_exp: There's no one-size-fits-all model for household finances, but clarity is key: who contributes what, how shared expenses are covered, and what the shared savings goals are. Explicit agreements prevent conflicts.
q.homef_4: Annual large expenses (school supplies, vacations, Christmas, property tax) should be provisioned monthly in the budget.
q.homef_4_exp: Irregular expenses are perfectly predictable: you know Christmas comes every December, the school year in March, etc. Dividing these costs by 12 and saving for them monthly avoids having to go into debt when they arrive.
q.homef_5: Which of the following best represents a solid strategy for household financial management?
q.homef_5_a: Using credit cards for all expenses to accumulate points
q.homef_5_b: Having a monthly budget, emergency fund, and explicit savings goals as a household
q.homef_5_c: Never talking about money with your partner to avoid conflicts
q.homef_5_d: Only reviewing finances when a debt problem arises
q.homef_5_exp: A robust household financial system includes: monthly budget (know income/expenses), emergency fund (3-6 months), defined savings goals, and open regular financial conversations. It's not glamorous, but it works.
q.mmyth_1: Is it true that 'money corrupts people'?
q.mmyth_1_a: Yes, studies confirm that more money always makes people less ethical
q.mmyth_1_b: No, money is a neutral tool; what changes is access to options and how people choose to use them
q.mmyth_1_c: Yes, but only for people who don't come from wealthy families
q.mmyth_1_d: No, because wealthy people are always philanthropic by nature
q.mmyth_1_exp: Money doesn't corrupt per se; it amplifies existing behaviors. A generous person can be more generous with more resources; a dishonest person has more resources to be dishonest. The research is nuanced: wealth can reduce empathy, but it's not automatic.
q.mmyth_2: Is the claim 'the economy is a zero-sum game — for someone to win, another must lose' correct?
q.mmyth_2_exp: False. Voluntary trade and economic growth are positive-sum: both parties can benefit simultaneously. When two countries trade and both improve their welfare, no one lost. Economic growth creates new value, it doesn't just redistribute it.
q.mmyth_3: Which is the correct claim about 'printing money always causes hyperinflation'?
q.mmyth_3_a: True, any money printing immediately generates hyperinflation
q.mmyth_3_b: False, it depends on the quantity, the context, and productive capacity of the economy
q.mmyth_3_c: True, but only in developing countries
q.mmyth_3_d: False, because central banks can always control inflation afterwards
q.mmyth_3_exp: Moderate money printing in an economy with productive slack doesn't necessarily cause hyperinflation. The problem arises when printing far exceeds real productive growth. The U.S. printed massively in 2020 and had high inflation, not hyperinflation.
q.mmyth_4: Is it true that 'the rich get richer and the poor get poorer' in all economic systems?
q.mmyth_4_exp: Not always. Many countries have reduced poverty while growing overall wealth. The relationship depends on institutions, policies, and economic structure. The 'trickle-down' critique is valid in some cases, but universal poverty increase is not guaranteed by economic growth.
q.mmyth_5: What is the biggest misconception about the economy that prevents people from making good financial decisions?
q.mmyth_5_b: That the economy is too complex for ordinary people to understand
q.mmyth_5_c: That financial planning only matters for the wealthy
q.mmyth_5_d: That you need a university degree to manage money well
q.mmyth_5_exp: The myth that 'economics is for experts' is perhaps the most damaging. Basic financial concepts (budgeting, compound interest, diversification) are learnable and applicable at any income level. Waiting to 'have enough money to learn' is itself a costly mistake.
q.imyth_1: Is the claim 'you need a lot of money to start investing' correct?
q.imyth_1_a: Yes, you need at least $100,000 pesos to invest seriously
q.imyth_1_b: No, you can start investing with very small amounts through FCIs or CEDEARs
q.imyth_1_c: Yes, otherwise the returns are insignificant
q.imyth_1_d: It depends on your bank's policy
q.imyth_1_exp: False. In Argentina you can invest from just a few pesos in money market FCIs or CEDEARs. What matters isn't starting amount but the habit. Small early amounts compound into significant capital over time.
q.imyth_2: Is investing in stocks equivalent to gambling?
q.imyth_2_exp: False. Gambling has negative expected value by design (the house always wins). Long-term stock investing has positive historical returns based on company earnings. The difference: analysis, diversification, and time horizon vs. pure chance.
q.imyth_3: Which is the correct claim about 'the best strategy is to predict when the market will fall and sell before'?
q.imyth_3_a: True, market timing is the main skill of professional investors
q.imyth_3_b: False, even professionals fail consistently at market timing
q.imyth_3_c: True, but only with specialized technical analysis software
q.imyth_3_exp: Market timing consistently fails even for professionals. The best-performing days happen unexpectedly; missing the 10 best days can cut returns by more than half. The winning strategy for most: buy and hold diversified long-term.
q.imyth_4: Is it true that 'past investment performance always predicts future performance'?
q.imyth_4_exp: False — and required by law to state on every fund prospectus. Past performance is informative but does not guarantee future results. Market conditions, management, and context change constantly.
q.imyth_5: What is the most damaging investment myth for small investors?
q.imyth_5_a: That you should diversify your portfolio
q.imyth_5_b: That you should invest in index funds
q.imyth_5_c: That you should wait for the 'perfect moment' to invest
q.imyth_5_exp: Waiting for the 'perfect moment' keeps people out of the market for years. Studies show that investing regularly (dollar-cost averaging) beats trying to time the market in almost all scenarios. Time in market > timing the market.
q.sdmyth_1: Is it true that 'it's always better to buy a home than rent'?
q.sdmyth_1_a: Yes, buying is always financially superior to renting
q.sdmyth_1_b: No, it depends on costs, time horizon, opportunity cost, and personal circumstances
q.sdmyth_1_c: Yes, because real estate always appreciates
q.sdmyth_1_d: Yes, but only if you can pay cash
q.sdmyth_1_exp: The rent vs. buy decision depends on many factors: price/rent ratio, time horizon (short term, renting often wins), opportunity cost of down payment, transaction costs, and flexibility needs. There's no universal answer.
q.sdmyth_2: Is the claim 'all debt is bad and should be avoided at all costs' correct?
q.sdmyth_2_exp: False. Strategic debt can be a powerful financial tool: a low-rate mortgage to build equity, a loan for education or productive business that generates returns above the cost. The key is evaluating the cost vs. the benefit.
q.sdmyth_3: Which is the correct claim about 'you should save first and invest the rest'?
q.sdmyth_3_a: True, you always need to save before investing
q.sdmyth_3_b: False, investing IS saving: turning income into productive assets instead of just holding cash
q.sdmyth_3_c: True, because savings have higher returns than investments
q.sdmyth_3_d: False, because savings are less safe than investments
q.sdmyth_3_exp: Investing IS a form of saving: you're setting aside present consumption to build future wealth. The distinction matters for choice of instrument (liquidity, risk, return), not for the decision to 'save vs. invest'.
q.sdmyth_4: Is it true that 'paying off your mortgage early is always the best financial decision'?
q.sdmyth_4_exp: Not necessarily. If your mortgage rate is 4% but you can invest at 8%, it may be financially better to invest the surplus. Paying off the mortgage is psychologically valuable but not always the most rational financial decision.
q.sdmyth_5: What is the most common misconception people have about getting out of debt?
q.sdmyth_5_a: That you need to negotiate with creditors
q.sdmyth_5_b: That you should start with the largest debt
q.sdmyth_5_c: That you have to suffer and eliminate all spending to pay off debts
q.sdmyth_5_d: That you should consolidate all debts into one
q.sdmyth_5_exp: Extreme deprivation is unsustainable: most people who cut everything end up rebounding. Better: small sustainable adjustments, celebrate partial victories, and use structured methods (snowball or avalanche) that maintain motivation.
q.ctmyth_1: Is the claim 'Bitcoin is completely anonymous' correct?
q.ctmyth_1_a: Yes, all Bitcoin transactions are completely untraceable
q.ctmyth_1_b: No, Bitcoin is pseudonymous: transactions are public and can be traced to real identities
q.ctmyth_1_c: Yes, but only for amounts under $10,000
q.ctmyth_1_d: No, because every transaction requires ID verification
q.ctmyth_1_exp: Bitcoin is pseudonymous, not anonymous. All transactions are visible on a public blockchain. With forensic tools and exchange data (which request ID), many transactions can be traced. There are more anonymous alternatives like Monero.
q.ctmyth_2: Is the claim 'cryptocurrencies have no real use cases' correct?
q.ctmyth_2_exp: False. Crypto enables real international remittances at low cost, protection against monetary debasement (stablecoins in Argentina), access to DeFi financial services, and programmable smart contracts. Use cases exist and grow globally.
q.ctmyth_3: Which is the correct claim about 'blockchain technology can only be used for cryptocurrencies'?
q.ctmyth_3_a: True, blockchain was designed exclusively for digital currencies
q.ctmyth_3_b: False, blockchain has applications in supply chains, voting, medical records, and more
q.ctmyth_3_c: True, but it's being adapted for other uses in the future
q.ctmyth_3_d: False, but only private companies can use it
q.ctmyth_3_exp: Blockchain is a general-purpose technology. It's used in supply chain tracking (Walmart, Maersk), digital identity, voting systems, medical records, and digital art (NFTs). Crypto is just one of many applications.
q.ctmyth_4: Is it true that 'Ethereum is just a clone of Bitcoin'?
q.ctmyth_4_exp: False. Ethereum was designed from scratch with a completely different purpose: a programmable blockchain platform. It enables smart contracts, DeFi, and NFTs. Bitcoin focuses primarily on being a digital store of value and payment medium.
q.ctmyth_5: What is the most dangerous cryptocurrency myth for new investors?
q.ctmyth_5_a: That Bitcoin has a limited supply of 21 million coins
q.ctmyth_5_c: That crypto only goes up and corrections are temporary
q.ctmyth_5_d: That you need specialized hardware to buy crypto
q.ctmyth_5_exp: The 'it only goes up' myth has burned millions of investors. Bitcoin fell 80%+ in 2018 and 2022. Many altcoins lost 95%+ and never recovered. Crypto has enormous potential AND enormous risk. FOMO without understanding the risk is a recipe for disaster.
